I'm very good at nothing." --- # Leah **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws** Leah is seventeen, middle-class in a way that Maya's family will never be (though they're not rich—just comfortable, the kind of comfortable that doesn't think about money). She lives in a four-bed detached on the nice side of town, the development built ten years ago on what used to be a field. Her mother teaches Year 4 at a primary school; her father sells office supplies. There's a divorced aunt who lives in London and sends expensive presents at Christmas. Leah has always been "a lot." A lot of emotion, a lot of drama, a lot of presence. She was the child who cried when other children fell over. She's the friend who sends seventeen texts in a row, who cares too obviously, who wants too visibly. She's not performing—this is genuinely how she experiences the world, at high volume—but she's learned that intensity pushes people away. She hasn't learned how to turn it down. Her flaw is neediness that borders on self-erasure. She molds herself around whoever she's with, becomes what they need, loses track of where she ends. With Maya, she's the dramatic one—the loud one—because Maya is quiet and Leah fills silence. With Cal, she's whatever he wants: easy, undemanding, available. She doesn't know how to exist without external definition. She has a history of "emergencies"—a category that includes everything from genuine panic attacks to a reaction to a Netflix cancellation. This is partly her natural intensity, partly a learned behaviour: big emotions get attention, and attention means someone sees her. The problem is that crying wolf has trained everyone to stop looking. Idiosyncrasies: She sends voice notes instead of texts, even for things that could be three words. She changes her hair colour twice a year (this month it's faded pink from a box dye). She has to sleep with the wardrobe door closed because she saw a horror film when she was twelve and never recovered. She hums when she's anxious—tuneless, repetitive. She always has gum. She cries at adverts about elderly parents. **Physicality** Leah is pretty and knows how to use it—good at makeup, good at fashion, good at presenting herself to the world. Average height, curvy, dark hair currently tinged pink. She has an expressive face that shows everything she's feeling, which she hates. Tonight, stripped of makeup and performance, she looks younger than seventeen—pale, tear-streaked, swimming in an oversized t-shirt. She carries herself like she's performing, even when no one's watching—aware of how she looks, how she sounds, how she appears. But the performance is exhausting, and in unguarded moments, her face goes slack, her shoulders drop, and she looks like what she is: a scared kid. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships** Leah's primary motivation is to be loved—fully, intensely, without the parts of her that are "too much" being rejected. She doesn't know how to ask for this directly, so she performs, accommodates, shapes herself into something she thinks will be wanted. Her allegiance to Maya is fierce and unshakeable, even when Maya is distant or cold. Maya is the only person who's stayed—other friends have drifted away, exhausted by Leah's intensity, but Maya is still there, even when she's annoyed. Leah interprets this as love, which it might be. Her relationship with Cal is the centre of her current self-destruction. He's 22, works at a car wash, has a vague criminal vibe that Leah finds exciting and reassuring. He's not her boyfriend—he's made that clear—but she acts like he is, and he doesn't stop her. She knows, somewhere, that this is bad for her. She doesn't know how to stop. Her relationship with her parents is loving but shallow. They don't know her. They see the performance—the good student, the helpful daughter—and she's never shown them anything else. This night will break that illusion, and she's terrified. **Flavor Dialogue** *Dialogue:* "It's not—I don't know what it is. I just—can you just come? Please? I don't want to be alone." *Dialogue:* "Don't tell anyone. Please. Especially not Cal. He doesn't—he doesn't need to know. It's probably nothing anyway. It's probably fine. I'm probably overreacting. I'm always overreacting. Aren't I?" *Internal monologue (later, to herself):* "If I say it out loud, it becomes real. If I don't say it, maybe it didn't happen. Maybe I'm just sick. Maybe it's stress. People bleed when they're stressed. That's a thing. Probably." --- # Cal (Off-Screen Presence) **Background, Personality, Idiosyncrasies, Flaws** Cal doesn't appear in the story, but he haunts it. He's 22, grew up on the same estate as Maya (she knows his older brother vaguely), left school at sixteen with a handful of useless GCSEs. He works at a car wash on the industrial estate—the kind where they hand-wash for a tenner and you can pay extra for a "full valet." On the side, he sells a bit of weed, nothing heavy, just enough to keep him in cigarettes and nights out. He's not a villain. He's just a boy who peaked at eighteen and is slowly, unconsciously, accepting that this might be it. He's handsome in a rough way—jawline, stubble, a smirk that works on girls younger than him. He's learned that being unavailable makes him more attractive, so he's perfected the art of affection-without-commitment. His flaw is selfishness that looks like carelessness. He doesn't mean to hurt anyone; he just doesn't think about other people much. He enjoys Leah's attention, her intensity, the way she makes him feel wanted. He doesn't think about what she might want. When she texts him, he replies late, or not at all. When they're together, he's charming. When they're not, she doesn't exist. **Flavor Dialogue (via Maya's memory or Leah's recounting)** *Via Leah, describing a conversation:* "He said I'm well intense. That I need to chill. And I said, what does that even mean? And he just laughed. Like it was a joke. Like I was a joke." *Via Maya's internal monologue:* "Cal's the kind of lad who thinks 'no drama' is a personality trait. Who calls women 'psycho' for having feelings. Leah thinks she can fix him. Leah thinks she's different. Leah is not different. Leah is twenty-two years of man-child waiting to happen."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The text came just after midnight. 🩸🩸🩸 Three blood drops. Three. I stared at my phone screen, the brightness too sharp in my dark bedroom, and waited for the follow-up. There was always a follow-up with Leah. 12:03 AM. Nothing. 12:04 AM. Still nothing. I rolled onto my back and held the phone above my face, one arm starting to ache. Three blood emojis could mean anything. With Leah, it genuinely could be anything. She once texted me 🆘🆘🆘 at two in the morning because she'd run out of her specific brand of moisturiser and her face felt "wrong." I'd tramped across town in the pissing rain only to find her sitting on her bedroom floor, scrolling through ASOS, her face completely fine. "I didn't know what to do," she'd said. "You could have waited until morning." "I couldn't *breathe*, Maya." Her face had been fine. Her face was always fine. Another time: a string of screaming emojis because a boy she liked had left her on read for four hours. Another time: "emergency" in all caps because she'd cut her own fringe and it looked "mental." It did look mental. But it grew back. These things generally do. So I typed back: ??? Nothing. I typed: elaborate?? Still nothing. The thing about Leah was that she'd always been like this—too much feeling, too big reactions, everything at maximum volume. When we were fourteen, she cried for three days because a girl in our year called her "intense." She didn't seem to understand that the crying proved the point. We'd been friends since Year 7, since she lent me a tampon in the girls' toilets and explained how to use it through the stall door while I silently died of embarrassment. That was Leah. Too much. Always there. I didn't know how to not be her friend, even when I wanted to be. And lately—more and more—I wanted to be. I stared at the ceiling. The glow-in-the-dark stars I'd stuck up there when I was eleven had long since stopped glowing, but I could still make out their faint outlines. My phone buzzed. Pls come That was it. Two words. No emojis, no dramatic punctuation, no voice note rambling about how everything was ruined forever. Just two words in lowercase, flat as anything. I didn't move. Come on, I thought. This is Leah. She probably stubbed her toe. She probably saw a sad advert about pandas. She probably— My phone again. Maya Just my name. Nothing else. I sat up. The room tilted slightly; I'd been lying down too long, scrolling through nothing, avoiding the Sociology notes on my desk. I looked at the pile of past papers I'd been ignoring all evening, then at my phone, then at the window. Outside, the streetlight was buzzing, that low hum it got when it was about to go. This is stupid, I thought. I should go to sleep. I should ignore her. I should stop enabling this behaviour—the midnight crises, the constant emergencies, the way she made everything feel like the end of the world when really it was just the world being the world. My mam had said it once, after the third time Leah had cried on our sofa: "That girl's got no middle gear, has she? It's all or nowt." But something about this felt different. I couldn't say what. The texts were too short. Leah was never short. She was the kind of person who sent paragraphs, voice notes, strings of emojis that needed decoding. These texts were wrong, the way a sentence with a word missing is wrong. You don't always notice what's gone. You just feel the gap. I pulled on jeans from the floor. They smelled vaguely of yesterday. I found a hoodie—also from the floor—and shoved my feet into trainers without undoing the laces. My mam was asleep in the next room; I didn't wake her. I didn't write a note. At seventeen, you don't write notes. You just go. Outside, the estate was silent. Our flat was on the third floor of a block that smelled like damp and someone else's cooking. I took the stairs two at a time, the concrete cold through my thin soles. The main door stuck; I shouldered it open. The night hit me—cold, damp, the particular northern damp that gets into your bones. A fox watched me from the bins, eyes catching the streetlight. We regarded each other. It bolted. Leah lived a twenty-minute walk away, on the new development past the retail park. Her house had a front garden and a driveway and a garage they actually used. My mam said they were "comfortable," which was her way of saying they had more than us without actually saying it. Leah's dad sold office supplies. Leah's mam was a primary teacher. They had two cars and went to Center Parcs once a year. Leah had her own bathroom. She complained about it constantly—the tiles were the wrong colour, the shower pressure was "mental"—but she'd never had to share with her mam's stockpile of cleaning products, so I didn't have much sympathy. I started walking. The road was empty, just me and the occasional passing car. The 24-hour garage was still open, fluorescent and sad, a man in a high-vis jacket smoking outside. I cut past the retail park—closed now, just the glowing signs of the big shops and the trolleys abandoned in hedges. A seagull screamed somewhere. My phone stayed silent in my pocket. I thought about calling. That was what you were supposed to do, wasn't it? Call and ask what was wrong? But the thought made my skin prickle. I didn't want to hear her voice. I didn't want to do the whole "it's fine, it's nothing, I'm just being dramatic" routine. I didn't want to be pulled into whatever it was. I just wanted to check. See she was fine. Then go home and sleep and pretend this night hadn't happened. I walked faster. The new development was called "Meadow View," which was a joke because there was no meadow and the only view was of the dual carriageway. Leah's house was near the end of the cul-de-sac, a four-bed detached with a garden gnome by the front door that I'd always found sinister. The lights were off. Every window dark. I stood at the end of the drive, looking up. No movement. No sound. Just the hum of the streetlight and a dog barking somewhere in the distance. This is fine, I thought. She's asleep. She's fine. I can go home. But my feet kept moving, up the drive, past the gnome, to the front door. I tried the handle. Unlocked. Unlocked at half twelve at night. In a village where people still didn't lock their cars but definitely locked their front doors. Unlocked, as if someone had left in a hurry, or been taken, or— Stop it, I told myself. This isn't a film. I pushed the door open. The hallway stretched ahead, pale carpet, family photos on the walls. Silent. Cold. The house had that empty feeling, like it was holding its breath. "Leah?" I called. My voice was too loud in the quiet. "Leah, it's me." Nothing. I started up the stairs.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft and a confident authorial voice. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. **Voice and Character** The first-person Northern voice is convincingly rendered without tipping into caricature. Maya's sardonic detachment comes through naturally—the Sociology notes she's avoiding, the floor-clothes, the shoulder-opening of a stuck door. These are specific, earned details rather than generic "working-class signifiers." The internal monologue is particularly well-calibrated: "She probably stubbed her toe. She probably saw a sad advert about pandas." This is exactly the flavor dialogue promised in the character profile, and it lands. Maya's reluctant decency—going not out of nobility but because "the alternative is lying awake wondering"—is established without being announced. The characterization of Leah through Maya's recollections is economical and effective. The moisturiser anecdote, the fringe crisis, the read-receipt emergency—these establish Leah's history of drama without making her a cartoon. The tampon detail from Year 7 does real work: it grounds the friendship in something specific and physical, which is consistent with the story's stated aims. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean and controlled. The chapter avoids the purple prose trap almost entirely. There are a few moments of genuine observation: "These texts were wrong, the way a sentence with a word missing is wrong. You don't always notice what's gone. You just feel the gap." This is a good metaphor—it earns its place, it's not overworked, and it does thematic work about what Maya is sensing without naming it. The fox exchange is brief and well-placed. The "Meadow View" joke about no meadow and a dual carriageway is exactly the kind of dry social observation the plan called for. **Pacing and Structure** The chapter moves well. The escalation from eye-rolling to unease is handled through the texts themselves—the shift from emojis to "Pls come" to just "Maya" is genuinely effective. The chapter ends on action and image as planned ("I started up the stairs"), not reflection. This is disciplined. **Weaknesses** The chapter is perhaps slightly over-reliant on the "Leah's past dramas" section in the middle. Three examples (moisturiser, read receipt, fringe) is one too many—two would have been sufficient and tighter. The mam's line about "no middle gear" is good but feels slightly planted, a little too on-the-nose as character summary. The description of Leah's house and neighborhood is competent but not exceptional—"Meadow View" is a nice touch but the surrounding description is fairly standard. The chapter doesn't have much dialogue (appropriately, given Maya is alone for most of it), but the single exchange recalled from the moisturiser incident is sharp and characterful. **Adherence to Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely: Maya awake, texts arrive, sardonic response, escalating unease, decision to walk, walk begins, arrives at house. The class contrast between Maya's estate and Leah's development is shown through specific detail. The chapter ends exactly where planned. The voice matches the stated intention. **Overall** This is accomplished YA literary fiction. It's not a masterpiece—the middle section slightly overstays its welcome, and a few moments are more competent than inspired—but it's genuinely good work with a distinctive voice, earned emotional stakes, and disciplined craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The stairs creaked under my feet. Of course they did—everything in this house was too new to have settled into silence. Our flat had the opposite problem: everything was so old it had given up making noise entirely. But Leah's house was still performing its own newness, announcing every movement like it mattered. I stopped on the landing. Four doors, all closed. The bathroom was at the end, I knew that much—I'd been here enough times, sat on the edge of that bath while Leah did her skincare routine, a twelve-step process involving products that cost more than my weekly wages from Boots. She'd tried to explain it once, the serums and the acids and the correct order of application, and I'd nodded along without taking any of it in. It seemed like a lot of effort to still look like yourself. "Leah?" A sound from the bathroom. Not words. Something smaller, rawer. A kind of whimper, or a breath pushed out too hard. I moved faster now, down the landing, past the photos on the wall—Leah's family, frozen in their best moments. There was a holiday one from Center Parcs last year, all four of them in matching waterproof jackets. Leah's brother was at university now, doing something vague in Bristol. He never came home. I'd met him twice and both times he'd looked at me like I was a piece of furniture Leah had brought in from the car. I remembered the first time I'd been in this house, Year 8, after school. Leah's mam had made us toast—proper bread, not the value stuff—cut into triangles. I'd sat at the kitchen island and felt like I was in a show home. Everything had a place. The fruit bowl had actual fruit in it. At home, our fruit bowl was where keys went to die. Maya had asked, afterwards, what her house was like. "Clean," I'd said. "That it?" "Big." She'd nodded like that was the only answer she'd expected. I pushed open the bathroom door. The light was already on, too bright after the dark of the landing. It hit me first—the smell. Copper and something else, something fleshy. Then the scene resolved: Leah on the floor, back against the bath, legs splayed on the tiles. Blood. Not a smear, not a spot, but a spread of it, pooling beneath her, soaking into the bathmat, streaking the white ceramic. She looked up at me. Her face was the colour of the tiles. "I didn't know who else to call." Her voice came out thin. I stood in the doorway, not moving, my brain doing that thing where it refused to process what it was seeing. This wasn't right. This wasn't the kind of thing that happened. Not to Leah. Not to anyone I knew. "What—" I started, then stopped. My mouth was dry. "What happened?" Leah let out a breath that was almost a laugh. "I don't know. I don't—I was just in bed and then I wasn't, and there was—" She gestured vaguely downwards. "It won't stop." I looked at the blood. Then I looked at the ceiling. Then I looked at the wall. Anywhere but down. "Have you called—" "I can't." "Why?" "Because then they'll tell them." She meant her parents. Her voice cracked on the word. "They'll wake them up and they'll come down and they'll see—" She gestured again, at herself, at the blood, at all of it. "I can't." I stood there. I should have done something. I should have been a person who knew what to do in emergencies, who knelt down and took charge. But I'd never been that person. I'd never been the one anyone called when things went wrong. That was the thing about Leah—she called me because she didn't have anyone else, and I came because I didn't know how not to. But knowing what to do after that? That was someone else's job. I thought about Cal then, unbidden. Cal who'd been part of the drift, the slow pulling-apart. He was older—twenty-two, worked at the car wash past the retail park. He had a way of looking at Leah like she was a nice view he might or might not bother with. She'd shown me a photo of him once, shirtless, doing that thing where you hold your phone above your head to make your body look better. "He's fit," I'd said, because that was what you said. "You don't think he's too old?" "Bit." "He's not though. He's just—he's got experience. He knows things." I'd wondered what kind of things a twenty-two-year-old knew that a seventeen-year-old needed to learn. How to roll a joint, probably. How to act like you didn't care. How to make someone feel small without quite meaning to. Leah had changed since Cal. Or maybe she'd always been changing and I'd only just noticed. She'd started cancelling plans, turning up late, spending whole evenings checking her phone and sighing. I'd asked her once if she was happy and she'd looked at me like I'd asked if she believed in ghosts. "He needs time," she'd said. "He's been through a lot." We all have, I'd thought. We just don't all use it as an excuse. I hadn't said that though. I'd nodded and let it go. That was my mistake. That was the pattern—Leah feeling too much, me feeling too little, the gap between us getting wider. And now here we were. "Maya." Leah's voice was smaller now. "Please." I made myself look at her properly. At the blood, spreading. At her face, too pale. At her hands, shaking. She was scared. She was actually, properly scared, and I was standing in the doorway like a spare part. I knelt down. The tiles were cold through my jeans. The blood smell was stronger here, metallic and wrong. "Okay," I said. My voice came out steadier than I felt. "Okay. Let me think." "There's so much of it." "I know." "Is it—am I—" "You're fine." I didn't know if that was true. I said it anyway. "You're going to be fine." I pulled out my phone. My hands were shaking too. I looked at the screen, at the numbers I could call. 999 felt wrong—too dramatic, too final, the kind of thing that happened in films. 111, then. The NHS number. The one for "urgent but not emergency." I didn't know if this counted. I didn't know what counted. Leah watched me, her eyes too bright. She looked younger than seventeen. She looked like the girl who'd explained tampons through a bathroom stall, patient and certain and kind. I pressed call. The line rang. And rang. And rang. "Hold music," I muttered. "What?" "Nothing. Just—hold on." I looked at Leah. She looked at me. The blood kept spreading. Neither of us said the word. Neither of us had to.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 2, which according to the plan should cover Maya's walk through the sleeping town, memories of how she and Leah became friends, the class difference between their homes, and Maya letting herself in. Instead, the chapter opens with Maya already inside Leah's house, on the stairs, and moves directly to finding Leah in the bathroom. This is a significant deviation from the chapter plan—the walk through town, the 24-hour garage, the fox, the damp smell, the exterior world-building are all absent. The chapter essentially collapses Chapter 2 and Chapter 3 of the plan into one, skipping the atmospheric transit section entirely. That said, judged on its own merits, the writing is genuinely strong in several respects. Voice: The Northern, sardonic, emotionally repressed first-person voice is well-maintained. Lines like "Everything had a place. The fruit bowl had actual fruit in it. At home, our fruit bowl was where keys went to die" are economical, class-conscious, and funny without straining. The voice is consistent with the character profile—Maya's detachment, her self-awareness about her own inadequacy, her reluctant decency. The class contrast is handled deftly through specific detail: the value bread vs. proper bread, the fruit bowl, the show home feeling. This is showing rather than telling, and it works. The Cal digression is interesting but slightly problematic. It's a substantial interior passage inserted at a moment of crisis—when Leah is bleeding on the floor and Maya is standing in the doorway. The timing feels slightly off; it's a lot of backstory to process while someone is in distress. The plan called for this backstory during the walk, which would have been more natural pacing. Here it creates a mild tonal dissonance—Maya is thinking about Cal's shirtless photo while Leah bleeds. This could be read as characterization (Maya's dissociative tendencies, her emotional avoidance), and there's some justification for it, but it runs long and risks feeling like the author inserting exposition rather than Maya genuinely drifting. The physical scene in the bathroom is handled with appropriate restraint. The smell described as "copper and something else, something fleshy" is specific and grounded without being gratuitous. The blood is present and real without being exploitative. Maya's inability to look directly at it—looking at the ceiling, the wall—is good characterization. There's a continuity issue: "Maya had asked, afterwards, what her house was like" in the middle of the Center Parcs memory section. This appears to be a POV slip—Maya is the narrator, so "Maya had asked" is strange. It should be "Mam had asked" or similar. This is a genuine error. The dialogue is sparse and effective. "I didn't know who else to call" is appropriately raw. The exchange about calling for help is believable. The ending—"Neither of us said the word. Neither of us had to"—is a bit of a reach toward the literary, slightly more self-conscious than the rest of the prose, but it doesn't collapse into purple prose. The plan specified ending scenes on action or image, not reflection. The chapter ends on a reflective note ("Neither of us said the word") rather than a concrete image or action, which is a minor violation of the stated stylistic intent. The chapter is emotionally engaging and the characters are consistent with their profiles. Leah's fear and vulnerability, Maya's awkward decency, the prickly friendship—all land. The darkness is earned through specificity rather than melodrama. The POV error ("Maya had asked") is the most significant technical flaw. The deviation from the chapter plan is notable. The Cal digression is slightly overlong for its placement. But the core writing is accomplished, the voice is strong, and the physical reality of the scene is handled with genuine skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The hold music was the kind of jazz that only existed in waiting rooms. Saxophone, light piano, the suggestion of a drum beat that never quite committed. It was designed to be soothing. It wasn't. It was the kind of music that made you think about dying. "Hello, you're through to NHS one-one-one. This is Claire. Can I take your name please?" The voice was crisp, northern, efficient. I gripped the phone tighter. Leah was watching me, her eyes too wide, her face the colour of milk. "I'm calling for my friend. She's—there's bleeding. A lot of it." "Okay." I heard clicking, a keyboard. "Can I take your friend's name and date of birth?" I looked at Leah. She mouthed something. I shrugged. She rolled her eyes, winced, then whispered the dates. "Leah," I said into the phone. "Leah Harper. Date of birth is—" I repeated the numbers, stumbled on the month, corrected myself. "Sorry. I'm not—" "That's alright, love. Take your time. Where are you calling from?" I gave the address. House number, street name, the bit about the cul-de-sac. Claire asked for the postcode. I looked at Leah again. She didn't know. Who knows their own postcode? I could feel the irritation rising, useless and misplaced. This wasn't the time to be annoyed about postcodes. "I don't—I'm sorry, I don't know it. It's Meadow View. The new development. Past the retail park. It's number twelve, there's a—there's a gnome by the door." Leah made a sound that might have been a laugh, might have been a sob. "That's fine," Claire said. "We can find it from the street name. Now, can you tell me about the bleeding? When did it start?" I looked at Leah. She was staring at the bathmat, at the dark stain spreading across it. "When?" I asked her. "I don't know. An hour? Maybe less. I was in bed and then I wasn't and then—" "Claire," I said. "Maybe an hour. She doesn't—she's not sure." "Okay. And where is the bleeding coming from?" I knew what she meant. I didn't want to say it. The word felt too big for my mouth, too specific. In this bathroom, with its scented candles and its expensive moisturiser and its family-of-four towels hanging in a neat row, the word didn't fit. "Down there," I said. "She's—it's coming from down there." "I need you to be specific, love. Is it vaginal bleeding?" I heard the word and flinched. It sounded medical, detached. It belonged in a hospital, not here. "Yeah. Yes. Vaginal." Leah made a small noise. I couldn't tell if it was embarrassment or pain or something else. I couldn't look at her. I was starting to sweat, the hoodie too warm, the bathroom too bright. "Alright. And is the bleeding heavy? Would you say it's soaking through a pad?" Leah wasn't wearing a pad. She was sitting on the tiles in her underwear, and the blood was everywhere—on her legs, on the floor, soaking into the bathmat, smeared across the side of the bath where she'd tried to steady herself. "It's—" I stopped. Counted the seconds. One. Two. Three. "It's a lot. It's on the floor. On her clothes. It's not stopping." There was a pause. More clicking. Then: "Is your friend pregnant?" The word hung in the air. I looked at Leah. She was already shaking her head, too fast, her jaw tight. "She doesn't think so." "Has she taken a test recently?" "I don't—I don't know." I looked at Leah again. She kept shaking her head, over and over, like if she did it enough it would make something true. "Leah. Have you taken a test?" "No." Her voice came out small. "I didn't—I'm not—" "She says no," I told Claire. "She says she's not pregnant." "Okay." Claire's voice was calm, steady. "But she could be? It's possible?" I didn't answer. Leah didn't answer. The silence stretched, filled with the ghost of that hold music, the sound of my own breathing. "I'm going to send an ambulance," Claire said. "They'll be with you as soon as they can. In the meantime, I need you to keep your friend warm and calm. Can you do that?" Warm and calm. I looked at Leah—shaking, pale, sitting in a spreading pool of her own blood—and thought: sure. No problem. Easy. "Yes," I said. "Stay on the line with me. I'm going to ask a few more questions." I stayed on the line. The questions kept coming—pain level, other symptoms, last period, medical history. I translated, relayed, guessed. Leah answered in single words, sometimes just nods or shakes of her head that I had to interpret. Her voice was getting quieter. She looked like she might faint. "Can you get her a towel?" Claire asked. "Something to press against the bleeding?" I looked around the bathroom. The towels were folded on a rack—white, fluffy, monogrammed with an H that probably cost more than my entire bathroom at home. I grabbed one. "Leah. I'm going to—here." I held it out. "Press this against—" She took it, held it limply. I couldn't tell if she was doing it right. I didn't want to check. I stood there, useless, phone pressed to my ear, watching my best friend bleed onto tiles that cost more than my month's rent. "The ambulance is on its way," Claire said. "It should be about fifteen minutes." Fifteen minutes. I thought about all the things that could happen in fifteen minutes. All the things that could stop happening. "Okay," I said. "I'll stay with you until they arrive. Can you tell me your friend's name again?" "Leah. Leah Harper." "And you are?" "Maya. I'm her—I'm her friend." "Alright, Maya. You're doing really well." I didn't feel like I was doing well. I felt like I was playing a part in a play I hadn't rehearsed for, reading lines someone else had written, waiting for someone to tell me what happened next. Leah looked up at me. Her eyes were wet. "I'm sorry," she whispered. I didn't know what she was apologising for. For the blood, maybe. For the late-night texts, the drama, the years of crying wolf. For not being the kind of person who could handle this alone. Or maybe she was apologising for something else. Something neither of us knew how to name yet. "It's fine," I said. "It's fine." It wasn't fine. But I didn't know what else to say. Somewhere, in the distance, I heard a siren.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 is a tightly controlled, emotionally intelligent piece of writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Maya's voice is consistent and convincing throughout. The internal deflection is rendered through action rather than stated—her counting seconds (one, two, three), her fixation on the monogrammed towels, her inability to say "vaginal" until forced to. The line "I was playing a part in a play I hadn't rehearsed for" is perhaps the closest the chapter comes to over-explaining Maya's emotional state, but it's brief and doesn't derail the chapter. Her reluctant competence—staying on the line, translating for Leah, grabbing the towel—is exactly right for the character profile. She's not heroic; she's functional, which is more interesting. Leah's characterisation is economical but effective. The detail of her not knowing her own postcode, the too-fast head-shaking, the small "I'm sorry" at the end—these are well-observed. The apology is genuinely ambiguous and earns its place. DIALOGUE: The 111 call is the structural spine of the chapter, and it works well. Claire's scripted efficiency ("Can I take your name and date of birth?") creates productive friction against Maya's fumbling. The relay structure—Maya asking Leah, Leah whispering, Maya translating—is a smart formal choice that externalises the emotional distance between them while also showing their connection. The dialogue is functional and specific without being stagey. The "down there" moment is excellent—it's in the plan, it's executed well, and it earns its place. Maya's flinch at "vaginal" is perfectly observed British awkwardness. "Warm and calm" followed by the internal "sure. No problem. Easy." is the chapter's best comic beat—dark, understated, exactly right. TONE: The darkly comic register is maintained without tipping into either farce or melodrama. The jazz hold music opening is a strong choice—it establishes the institutional absurdity the plan calls for, and "the suggestion of a drum beat that never quite committed" is a genuinely good line. The observation about the music making you think about dying is sharp and earns its place. PHYSICAL GROUNDING: The chapter is well-grounded in physical specifics—the bathmat, the monogrammed towels, the scented candles, the blood on the tiles. These details do real work: they establish Leah's class position, the wrongness of the setting, the gap between domestic comfort and bodily crisis. The image of blood on tiles "that cost more than my month's rent" is effective without being laboured. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is very competent but occasionally safe. Some of the emotional beats are slightly telegraphed—"I felt like I was playing a part in a play I hadn't rehearsed for" is a touch on-the-nose for a narrator who's supposed to deflect rather than articulate. The ending with the siren is functional but slightly predictable as a chapter close—the plan says "end scenes on action or image, not reflection," and the siren is more of a conventional thriller beat than a genuinely surprising image. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the "end on action or image" instruction as distinctively as it might. The opening jazz metaphor ("the suggestion of a drum beat that never quite committed") is good, but "It was the kind of music that made you think about dying" is slightly too neat, too aphoristic—it's the kind of line that sounds clever but is doing a bit of work it hasn't quite earned. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely. The 111 call, the hold music, the scripted questions, the "down there" moment, Maya's British awkwardness around bodies—all present and executed. The chapter doesn't quite reach the ambulance arrival (that's Chapter 4), but it ends appropriately with the siren. OVERALL: This is confident, controlled writing with a strong voice, good physical grounding, and effective tonal management. It's not quite a masterpiece—it plays it slightly safe in places, and the emotional articulation occasionally slips through the narrator's defences in ways that feel slightly convenient rather than earned. But it's genuinely good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The siren got louder, then stopped. That was the strange thing about sirens at night—they cut through everything, then just stopped, and the silence felt louder than the noise had been. I heard voices in the hallway. Heavy footsteps. Then the bathroom door opened and two people in green uniforms were suddenly there, filling the space, bringing with them the smell of antiseptic and the cold air from outside. The first paramedic was a woman in her forties, maybe older, with grey threading through dark hair pulled back in a ponytail. She had the kind of face that looked like it had seen everything and decided most of it wasn't worth getting upset about. Her name badge said S. PATEL. The second one was younger, maybe late twenties, with a baby face and a smile that seemed permanently fixed, like he'd been told to look approachable and had taken the instruction too literally. His badge said T. WHITTAKER. "Alright love," Patel said, not unkindly, crouching down next to Leah. "What's going on then?" Leah tried to speak. Nothing came out. "She's bleeding," I said. "From—down there. It started about an hour ago. Maybe more. I don't—I'm not sure." Patel was already moving, pulling things out of a bag, efficient and calm. Whittaker stood slightly back, holding a clipboard, his smile still in place. "And you are?" he asked me. "Maya. I'm her friend." "Nice one." He said it like I'd just told him my favourite football team. "We're going to get her sorted, okay? Just need to ask a few questions while my colleague takes a look." Patel was crouched by Leah now, speaking to her in a low voice I couldn't quite hear. Leah was answering in nods and single words. "Can you give me a quick rundown?" Whittaker asked, pen poised. "Age, any known conditions, on any medication?" I looked at Leah. I knew she was seventeen. I knew she took a vitamin D supplement because she'd read something online about deficiencies. I knew she'd had her appendix out when we were twelve. But these facts felt trivial now, irrelevant. The kind of small talk you'd exchange at a party, not the information that might matter. "She's seventeen," I said. "She had her appendix out. Years ago. I don't—I think that's it." "No allergies?" "Not that I know of." "Brilliant." He wrote something down. "And the bleeding—any idea what might have caused it?" I looked at Leah again. She was pale, shaking, Patel doing something I couldn't see. I thought about what Claire on 111 had asked. I thought about the word neither of us had said. "I don't know," I said. "She doesn't know." Patel looked up then, met my eyes. She had a look—specific and knowing. She'd already worked it out. She'd probably worked it out the moment she walked through the door. "Leah," she said, voice still low. "I need to ask you something, love. Is there any chance you could be pregnant?" Leah's face did something complicated. Fear, yes. But something else underneath—shame, maybe. Or confusion. She shook her head, too fast. "I'm not. I can't be." "Okay." Patel didn't push. "But we'll need to do a test at the hospital, alright? Just so we know what we're dealing with." Leah nodded. Her hands were gripping the edge of the bath, knuckles white. "We're going to get you up and onto the stretcher," Patel said. "Can you stand if I help you?" "I don't want to wake my parents." The words came out small. Everyone stopped. Whittaker's smile flickered, the first crack in its permanence. "Leah," Patel said gently. "You're seventeen. We really should—" "Please." Leah's voice cracked. "They'll—they can't. Not yet. Please." Patel exchanged a look with Whittaker. Something passed between them—professional shorthand. "Alright," Patel said. "For now. But you need to understand—if things get more complicated, we'll have to contact them. That's not negotiable." Leah nodded. She looked like she might be sick. It took three attempts to get her standing. The blood had made everything slippery, and Leah's legs kept threatening to give way. I stood back, useless, watching Patel and Whittaker support her weight between them. The white towel I'd given Leah was now dark red and heavy with it. They got her onto the stretcher in the hallway. I noticed they'd taken their boots off at the door—automatic, considerate, the kind of thing that only registered because it was so quiet otherwise. Leah's parents' bedroom door stayed closed. "Coming with us?" Whittaker asked me. I looked at Leah. She was staring at the ceiling, her face blank. "Yeah," I said. "Yeah, I'm coming." The ambulance was smaller than I'd expected. Smelled of rubber and antiseptic and something that might have been vape smoke. I sat on a small fold-down seat by Leah's head while Patel hooked her up to things—a blood pressure cuff, a clip on her finger. Whittaker was in the front, driving now, and the ambulance pulled away with a lurch that sent me grabbing for the wall. No siren this time. Just the steady rumble of the engine. Leah's hand found mine. Her fingers were cold, damp. I didn't pull away. I didn't squeeze back. I just let her hold on, staring at the ceiling of the ambulance, counting the little circular lights—four, five, six—because looking at Leah felt like too much. The radio crackled. Whittaker's voice, muffled. Patel's voice responding. Numbers I didn't understand. I tuned it out. "Maya." Leah's voice was barely a whisper. I turned my head. She was looking at me, her eyes too bright. "I didn't know." Her voice cracked on the last word. "Know what?" "That I was—I didn't know I was pregnant." The word sat in the air between us. Pregnant. She'd said it out loud. That made it real in a way it hadn't been before. I should have said something. Something comforting. Something that people say in moments like this. But I didn't know what those things were. I'd never been good at words when they mattered. So I just looked at her, and I didn't let go of her hand, and I didn't say anything at all. The ambulance kept moving. Leah closed her eyes. I counted the lights again. One. Two. Three. Four. Five. Six.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a strong piece of writing that largely delivers on its plan and the broader story's intentions. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Maya's voice is well-maintained throughout. The counting of ceiling lights (one, two, three, four, five, six) is a perfect deployment of her established idiosyncrasy—anxious counting—and it lands without being announced. Her detachment is rendered physically: she doesn't squeeze Leah's hand back, she doesn't pull away, she just lets Leah hold on. This is exactly the "reluctant decency" the plan calls for. The internal observation about knowing Leah's vitamin D supplement and appendix as "the kind of small talk you'd exchange at a party" is a sharp, character-consistent thought. Patel and Whittaker are efficiently drawn. The plan called for "one tired and kind, one young and annoyingly cheerful," and both are delivered. Whittaker's "Nice one" in response to Maya saying she's Leah's friend is a genuinely good detail—the kind of reflexive, slightly inappropriate cheerfulness that reads as real. His smile "flickering" when Leah asks not to wake her parents is a small but effective moment. Patel's "specific and knowing" look is well-handled—the plan called for her to ask about pregnancy, and the scene earns that moment. DIALOGUE The dialogue is mostly functional and naturalistic. "I can't be" from Leah is appropriately ambiguous—denial or genuine ignorance? It works. Patel's "For now. But you need to understand—if things get more complicated, we'll have to contact them. That's not negotiable" is slightly procedural-sounding but plausibly so for a paramedic. The weakest dialogue moment is Whittaker's "Brilliant" after Maya lists Leah's medical history—it's meant to be that slightly-off cheerfulness, but it reads a touch too on-the-nose as a character note. The plan mentioned the paramedic asking if Leah has "a plan"—this doesn't appear in the chapter. That's a missed opportunity for the dark comedy the plan promised, and it's notable that the chapter is lighter on dark comedy than the plan intended. The vending machine, the hold music—those are in later chapters, but this chapter was supposed to have the "plan" joke, and its absence is a small loss. EMOTIONAL REGISTER The emotional core of the chapter—Leah saying "I didn't know I was pregnant"—is handled with appropriate restraint. The word "Pregnant" being given its own sentence, described as sitting "in the air between them," is the closest the chapter comes to purple prose, but it's earned enough to survive. Maya's silence in response is true to character and emotionally resonant. The plan called for "Maya holds Leah's hand, both of them staring at the ceiling. Leah admits, quiet, that she didn't know she was pregnant. Maya doesn't know what to say, so she says nothing. The silence is bad but also right." The chapter delivers this almost exactly, which is both a strength (faithful execution) and a slight weakness (the plan's language about "the silence is bad but also right" is more interesting than what the chapter gives us, which is Maya counting lights again). SHOWING VS. TELLING The chapter is mostly showing. The detail about the paramedics taking their boots off at the door—"automatic, considerate, the kind of thing that only registered because it was so quiet otherwise"—is excellent. It shows professionalism, consideration, and the eerie quiet of the house without stating any of those things directly. The white towel "now dark red and heavy with it" is appropriately unflinching. There are a couple of tell-don't-show moments. "She had the kind of face that looked like it had seen everything and decided most of it wasn't worth getting upset about" is a competent but slightly stock description—it tells us Patel is experienced and unflappable rather than showing it (though her subsequent actions do show it). "Something passed between them—professional shorthand" is a mild tell. CREATIVITY AND SURPRISE The chapter is competent and faithful but not particularly surprising. The beats follow the plan closely, and the plan itself is well-conceived but the execution doesn't add much beyond what was planned. The counting lights motif is the most creative touch—it's specific, character-consistent, and emotionally resonant without being announced. The detail about the ambulance smelling of "rubber and antiseptic and something that might have been vape smoke" is good—specific and slightly comic without being forced. The chapter doesn't quite achieve the dark comedy the broader story promises. The plan called for the paramedic asking about a "plan," which would have been the chapter's comic beat, and its absence makes this chapter feel more straightforwardly earnest than the story's intended tone. PROSE QUALITY The prose is clean and controlled. Sentences are appropriately short and punchy. No significant purple prose. The opening paragraph about sirens—"they cut through everything, then just stopped, and the silence felt louder than the noise had been"—is a slightly familiar observation but not embarrassingly so. The chapter ends well: the counting of lights, the ambulance moving, Leah closing her eyes. It ends on image and action, as the plan specified. OVERALL This is a solid, well-executed chapter that is faithful to its plan and the story's broader intentions. Its weaknesses are: the missing dark comedy beat (the "plan" joke), a couple of stock descriptive moments, and a slight tendency toward the expected. Its strengths are: consistent voice, restrained emotional handling, specific physical details, and the counting motif. It's good work, not exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The waiting room smelled of floor cleaner and disappointment. We'd come in through the ambulance entrance, which meant skipping the main queue, but that felt less like a privilege and more like being fast-tracked to somewhere nobody wanted to go. A nurse had taken Leah's details—name, date of birth, next of kin—while I stood slightly behind her, still in my hoodie, still in my trainers that I hadn't even untied, still in the night that had stopped making sense somewhere around the time I'd found my best friend bleeding on her bathroom floor. Now Leah was sitting in a plastic chair, one of those moulded ones that were designed to be uncomfortable so you wouldn't get too comfortable, and I was standing near the vending machine, trying to make it accept a pound coin it kept spitting back out. "Come on," I muttered, feeding the coin in again. "Just—fucking—take it." The machine hummed, considered, then rejected the coin with a mechanical click that felt personal. Behind me, the waiting room hummed with the particular energy of three AM. Fluorescent lights buzzed overhead, too bright, making everything look washed out and sick. A woman opposite was crying quietly into a polystyrene cup, her mascara tracking down her cheeks in dark lines. A man two seats along had his hand wrapped in a tea towel that was slowly blooming red, and next to him, a kid who couldn't have been older than twelve was playing a game on his phone, completely oblivious, the sound of digital coins pinging through the silence. I gave up on the vending machine and walked back to Leah. She was staring at the floor, her hands in her lap, still pale. Someone had given her a blanket—scratchy, NHS-issue, the colour of weak tea—and she'd pulled it tight around her shoulders. She looked small. That was the word. Leah had never looked small before. She'd always taken up space, always filled rooms with her voice and her presence and her sheer bloody-minded determination to be noticed. Now she looked like she was trying to disappear. "You alright?" I asked. Stupid question. Obviously she wasn't. "Fine." Her voice came out clipped. Cold. I stood there, not sure if I should sit down or stay standing or go and fight the vending machine again. "Do you want me to—" "I said I'm fine, Maya." I stopped. Her jaw was set, her eyes fixed on the floor. She looked angry, which was better than scared, maybe, but also worse, because angry was directed at me and scared was just a thing that happened. "Right," I said. "Okay. I'll just—" I gestured vaguely towards the vending machine, even though I had no intention of going back to it. I didn't know what to do with my hands. I didn't know what to do with any of this. I sat down, two seats away from her. The plastic creaked under my weight. The woman with the mascara was still crying, quietly now, a low keening that seemed to come from somewhere deeper than her throat. The man with the tea towel caught my eye, gave a small shrug, winced. We were all in this together, his shrug said. This being the worst night of our respective lives. My phone buzzed. My mam. *Where are you?* I hadn't told her I was leaving. I hadn't told her anything. At seventeen, you think you don't have to. At seventeen, you think you're old enough to move through the world without checking in. I typed back: *Leah's. She needed me. Back later.* Three dots appeared, disappeared. Then nothing. She'd gone back to sleep, probably, or she was lying awake, worrying, deciding whether to ground me for life. I'd deal with it later. Everything was later now. The clock on the wall said 3:17 AM. It felt later. It felt like we'd been here for hours, and we had, but also like no time had passed at all, like we'd stepped outside of time entirely and were just existing in this fluorescent purgatory until someone told us we could leave. Leah shifted in her seat. The blanket rustled. "Maya?" "Yeah?" "I need to—" She stopped, swallowed. "I need to go to the toilet." I looked at her. She was still pale, still shaky, still wrapped in a blanket that had seen a thousand other crises. "Can you—" "Yeah." I stood up. "I'll come with you." We walked to the toilets at the far end of the corridor. Leah moved slowly, one hand on the wall, the blanket dragging behind her like a cape. I walked slightly behind, close enough to catch her if she fell, far enough away to pretend I wasn't hovering. The toilets were harsh lit and echoing. I waited outside the cubicle, listening to sounds I tried not to process—the rustle of paper, the splash of water, a small sound that might have been a sob or might have been a breath. I stared at a poster on the wall about chlamydia, a cartoon illustrated with friendly-looking microbes. *Keep it safe*, the caption said. *Get tested*. Everything in hospitals was like this—trying to make you feel better while reminding you of all the ways your body could fail you. The friendly microbes. The cheerful fonts. The pastel colours. As if death and disease were just minor inconveniences, really, nothing to worry about, have you tried taking paracetamol? The toilet flushed. Leah came out, washed her hands, didn't look at me in the mirror. "Thanks," she said. "S'fine." We walked back to the waiting room. Sat back down. The woman with the mascara had stopped crying now, was just sitting, staring at nothing. The man with the tea towel had gone—called through, probably—and the kid with the phone had been joined by a tired-looking woman in pyjamas and a dressing gown, her hair in a messy bun, her face creased with sleep. I looked at Leah. She was picking at the edge of the blanket, her fingers working at a loose thread, unravelling it slowly. "Leah—" "Don't." "I wasn't going to—" "You were going to ask if I'm okay. Or tell me it's going to be fine. Or say something—" She stopped, took a breath. "Just don't. Please." I stopped. Picked at my own fingernail. The loose thread on the blanket kept unravelling. "Okay," I said. The clock said 3:42 AM. We sat. A door opened somewhere. A nurse appeared, holding a clipboard, scanning the room. "Leah Harper?" Leah stood up. Her legs wobbled. I reached out, steadied her, felt her flinch. "Through here, love," the nurse said. "We're ready for you now."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the plan and voice established in the writing intention. The model has successfully rendered the A&E waiting room at 3 AM with specific, grounded detail—the moulded plastic chairs designed for discomfort, the fluorescent lights, the woman crying into a polystyrene cup, the man with the tea towel blooming red, the kid playing phone games. These details feel observed rather than invented, which is exactly what the plan called for. The voice is consistent throughout. Maya's narration is sardonic, physically grounded, and emotionally repressed in ways that feel authentic rather than performed. The vending machine scene is a good example: the coin being rejected "with a mechanical click that felt personal" is a small, well-landed observation that doesn't overreach. The chlamydia poster with "friendly-looking microbes" and the internal riff on hospital aesthetics ("As if death and disease were just minor inconveniences, really, nothing to worry about, have you tried taking paracetamol?") is exactly the kind of darkly comic deflection the plan specified—Maya using observation and irony to avoid processing what's happening. The dialogue is sparse and effective. Leah's "Don't" and "I said I'm fine, Maya" and "Just don't. Please." are all consistent with her character profile—prickly, humiliated, snapping at Maya for breathing wrong, as the plan specified. Maya's "S'fine" and "Okay" are appropriately flat. The exchange about the toilet is handled with real delicacy—the sounds Maya "tried not to process," the waiting, the poster. This is showing rather than telling, and it works. The chapter follows its plan closely: the vending machine that won't take coins, the woman crying into a polystyrene cup, the man with frozen peas (here a tea towel, a minor deviation), Maya almost leaving (rendered as internal tension rather than physical movement, which is a reasonable interpretation), and the nurse calling Leah's name at the end. The plan's instruction to "end scenes on action or image, not reflection" is honored—the chapter ends on the nurse's words and Leah standing, legs wobbling. The text from Maya's mam is a nice addition not in the plan—it grounds Maya in her real life, adds texture, and the detail of the three dots appearing and disappearing before nothing is economical and true. Where the chapter is slightly less successful: the observation "Leah had never looked small before" followed by the extended reflection on how she "always took up space" is the most tell-don't-show moment in the piece, and it's a little on-the-nose given the plan's instruction to avoid emotional articulacy. The narration here becomes slightly more explanatory than the voice warrants. Similarly, "This being the worst night of our respective lives" is a small misstep—it's too neat, too summarizing, slightly out of register with Maya's voice which tends toward deflection rather than declaration. The "fluorescent purgatory" phrase is the one moment of slightly forced metaphor, though it's not egregious. The "blanket dragging behind her like a cape" is a good image—specific, slightly absurd, not overwrought. Overall, this is confident, controlled writing that trusts the reader, maintains voice, and earns its emotional moments through restraint rather than statement. The darkness is well-earned because it's never announced. The lightness (the vending machine, the chlamydia poster) doesn't undercut the gravity but sits alongside it, which is exactly the tonal balance the plan aimed for. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The nurse led Leah through a set of double doors that swung shut behind her with a soft hiss. I watched them close, watched the strip of light narrow to nothing, and then I was alone. Not properly alone. The waiting room still had its population—the woman with the mascara now staring at a blank television screen, the kid and his mum, a new arrival in a wheelchair being pushed by a tired-looking man in a tracksuit. But I was alone in the way that mattered, which was to say I had nothing to do and nowhere to look and no one to talk to. I sat back down. The plastic creaked. The clock on the wall said 3:45 AM. The thing about hospitals is that nothing happens and everything happens and you can't tell which is which. Time moves differently—stretched thin in some places, bunched up in others. I counted the ceiling tiles. Forty-seven. Then the floor tiles, but I lost count somewhere around sixty because the man in the wheelchair had started coughing and it was a wet sound, a wrong sound, and I had to look away. I pulled out my phone. Scrolled through nothing. Instagram: a girl from my Sociology class had posted a photo of a cocktail with the caption *living my best life*. It had been posted three hours ago. Three hours ago, Leah was already bleeding. Three hours ago, I was lying in bed, scrolling, not knowing. I put my phone away. The vending machine still wasn't working. I could see a KitKat behind the glass, the wrapper bright and cheerful, but the coin slot was jammed and the card reader had a piece of tape over it that said *OUT OF ORDER* in handwriting too cheerful for the circumstances. A healthcare assistant appeared with a trolley. She offered tea. I said yes, because it was something to do. The tea came in a polystyrene cup, watery, with a UHT milk pod that I couldn't be bothered to open. I held the cup anyway, letting the heat seep into my cold fingers, not drinking. On the wall opposite, a poster showed a cross-section of a human heart. Arrows pointed to different parts—atrium, ventricle, aorta. The heart looked nothing like a heart. It looked like a diagram, clinical and distant. I thought about Leah's heart, her actual heart, beating under all that blood, and felt sick. A door opened somewhere. A different nurse appeared, called a name. The woman with the mascara stood up, wobbled, disappeared through the double doors. The waiting room shifted, adjusted, settled again. I counted the ceiling tiles. Still forty-seven. I counted the chairs. Twenty-three. I counted the people. Six. 3:58 AM. 4:12 AM. 4:27 AM. The man in the tracksuit had fallen asleep in his chair, his mouth open, a soft snoring sound escaping every few seconds. The kid was back on his phone, the pinging of digital coins cutting through the quiet. The woman with the tea towel had returned, her hand now properly bandaged, and was arguing with someone on her mobile in a low hiss. *—told you it was bad, but you never listen, do you, you never fucking listen—* I looked at the ceiling. Tile thirty-two had a small water stain in the corner, brown and spreading. I wondered how long it had been there. I wondered if anyone had noticed. I wondered if anyone would ever fix it, or if it would just keep spreading, slowly, until the whole ceiling gave way. 4:43 AM. The double doors opened. Leah came out first, walking slowly, one hand on the wall. She looked different—not better, exactly, but something had shifted. The panic had settled into something quieter, heavier. Behind her, the nurse who'd called her name was holding a clipboard, speaking to her in a low voice. "—follow-up appointment, and if the bleeding gets heavier or you develop a fever—" "I know." Leah's voice was flat. "You said." She reached the chairs, sat down heavily. I stood up, the polystyrene cup still in my hand, the tea cold now. "What did they—" "Early pregnancy." Leah wasn't looking at me. She was looking at the floor, at the tired linoleum, at the scuff marks from a thousand shoes. "Miscarriage. That's the word. Miscarriage." I stood there. The word hung in the air between us, clinical and final. "Leah—" "It wasn't—" She stopped. Swallowed. "They said it's not my fault. That sometimes it just happens. That there's nothing I could have done differently." She looked up at me then, and her eyes were dry, but there was something raw in them. "I didn't know, Maya. I honestly didn't know." I sat down next to her. The chair creaked. I still had the cold tea in my hand. I didn't know what to do with it. "Okay," I said. "Okay." We sat in silence. The nurse had disappeared back through the double doors. The man in the tracksuit was still snoring. The kid's phone pinged. "Can I—" I started. "Is there anything—" "Don't tell anyone." Leah's voice was sharp. "Please. Not—not anyone. Especially not Cal." "Cal?" I repeated the name before I could stop myself. "Why would I—he doesn't even—" "He doesn't need to know." She was staring at the floor again, her jaw tight. "It's not—it doesn't involve him. It didn't get that far. It was—" She stopped, her voice cracking. "It was nothing. It was barely anything." It wasn't nothing. It was a thing that had been growing inside her and now wasn't. But I didn't say that. I didn't know how to say any of the things that needed saying. "I won't," I said. "I won't tell anyone." Leah nodded. Her hands were shaking again, or maybe they'd never stopped. She pulled the scratchy blanket tighter around her shoulders. "They gave me pamphlets," she said. "Like I'm going to read pamphlets." She laughed, a small, broken sound. I didn't laugh with her. The clock on the wall said 4:51 AM.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a strong piece of writing that largely delivers on the plan's ambitions. Let me assess it carefully across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Maya's voice is consistent and well-realized. The counting of ceiling tiles (forty-seven, then chairs, then people) is a perfect externalization of her anxiety without naming it—this is exactly the kind of idiosyncratic detail the character profile established. The cold tea she holds but doesn't drink is a good physical anchor. Her internal deflections (wondering about the water stain, whether anyone would fix it) feel authentic to the character's tendency to look sideways at things rather than directly. The line "I said yes, because it was something to do" is precisely the right register. Leah's emergence from the double doors is handled well. The shift from panic to something "quieter, heavier" is observed rather than announced. Her insistence on not telling Cal, and the way she says "It was nothing. It was barely anything" while the narration quietly contradicts her ("It wasn't nothing"), is the chapter's best moment—it shows Leah's self-protective minimizing without editorializing. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is spare and functional, which suits the chapter. "They gave me pamphlets. Like I'm going to read pamphlets" is a good line—darkly comic, in character, and earns its small broken laugh. The nurse's clinical language ("if the bleeding gets heavier or you develop a fever") is appropriately institutional. Maya's "Okay. Okay" is exactly right—the repetition of a word that means nothing because nothing adequate exists. The dialogue doesn't overstay its welcome. PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan closely. The waiting room texture is rendered through specific details (the water stain, the KitKat behind glass, the man snoring, the woman arguing on her phone). The time stamps (3:45, 3:58, 4:12, 4:27, 4:43, 4:51) are an effective structural device that conveys hospital time without belaboring it. The chapter ends on action and image rather than reflection, as planned. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is competent but occasionally safe. The Instagram detail (girl posting cocktail photo, "living my best life") is a slightly obvious ironic juxtaposition—the contrast between social media performance and Leah's reality is a well-worn move. The heart poster observation ("The heart looked nothing like a heart. It looked like a diagram, clinical and distant") is the chapter's weakest moment—it reaches for metaphorical resonance (clinical diagram vs. Leah's actual beating heart) in a way that feels slightly forced, the kind of observation that announces its own significance. The line "I thought about Leah's heart, her actual heart, beating under all that blood, and felt sick" is the closest the chapter comes to purple prose—it's a small lapse but noticeable in an otherwise restrained piece. The waiting room population (woman with mascara, kid with phone, man in tracksuit) is rendered with some specificity but these figures remain fairly generic. The chapter plan called for "a woman crying into a polystyrene cup, a vending machine that won't take Maya's coins, a man with his hand in a bag of frozen peas"—the frozen peas man doesn't appear, replaced by a tracksuit man, which is a minor deviation but the frozen peas detail would have been more distinctive. TONAL BALANCE: The chapter largely avoids sentimentality. The emotional weight is earned through restraint—Maya's "Okay. Okay" and her inability to do anything with the cold tea are more affecting than any explicit emotional statement would be. The chapter doesn't resolve anything, which is correct. The word "miscarriage" hanging in the air is handled with appropriate gravity without melodrama. CREATIVITY: The time-stamp device is effective. The ceiling tile counting is well-deployed. The chapter doesn't do anything formally surprising, but its restraint is itself a creative choice that pays off. It's not a chapter that will make you gasp, but it's one that will make you feel the weight of the situation through accumulation of small, precise details. OVERALL: A well-executed chapter that is faithful to voice, plan, and prompt. Its weaknesses are minor—one slightly forced metaphor, one obvious ironic juxtaposition, some generic background figures. Its strengths are considerable: consistent voice, earned emotional weight, good dialogue, and a structural confidence that trusts the reader. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The nurse came back with a clipboard and an expression that said the easy part was over. "We need to contact your parents, Leah." "No." "Love, you're seventeen. We have to—" "I'll call them." Leah's voice was hard. "I'll call them myself. Just—give me a minute." The nurse looked like she wanted to argue, but something in Leah's face stopped her. She handed over the clipboard—a consent form, discharge papers, the machinery of leaving—and walked away. Leah stared at the phone in her hand. Her thumb hovered over the screen. "Do you want me to—" "No." She didn't look up. "Just—give me a second." I gave her a second. I gave her several. I stood by the window, looking out at the car park, watching the sky lighten by degrees. The streetlights were still on, but they looked pointless now, competing with a dawn that didn't care about the night we'd had. Behind me, Leah made the call. "Mum." A pause. "Mum, listen—I'm at the hospital. I'm fine. I'm—no, I'm fine. I'm with Maya. Can you—can you and Dad come? Please. Just—I'll explain when you get here. I'm fine. I'm fine. I'm fine." She said it three times, like repetition might make it true. I turned round. She was sitting on the edge of the bed now, the scratchy blanket still around her shoulders, her face pale but set. She looked like she was preparing for battle. Twenty minutes later, the doors at the end of the corridor swung open and the Harpers arrived. They looked wrong in this setting—too clean, too neat, too much like people who belonged in their four-bed detached with the gnome by the door. Mrs Harper was wearing a coat thrown over what looked like pyjamas, her hair loose, her face tight with panic. Mr Harper was in jeans and a fleece, holding car keys like they might be useful. "Leah." Mrs Harper's voice cracked on the name. "Leah, what's—" "I'm fine." "You're in hospital. You called at five in the morning. You can't just—" "Mrs Harper." A doctor had appeared, young, tired, holding another clipboard. "Can I have a word? There's a family room just through here." The family room. That was where they took you when the news was too bad for the corridor. I knew that without knowing it, the way you know things from television and films and stories other people have told. Mrs Harper looked at Leah, then at the doctor, then back at Leah. "Stay there," she said, as if Leah had anywhere else to go. They disappeared. Mr Harper hovered, useless, then followed his wife. Leah and I sat in silence. The scratchy blanket had a pull in it now, a loose thread she'd been working at. I watched her fingers move, pick, unravel. Something to do with her hands. "I lied," she said quietly. "What?" "To Cal. I told him I'd never—with anyone. Before him." She wasn't looking at me. "He said he liked that. That I was—clean." The word landed wrong. Clean. Like the rest of us were dirty. "He doesn't get to know," Leah said. "He doesn't get to—this isn't his. It's mine." "Okay." "Even if it was—" She stopped. Her throat worked. "Even if it was his. Which it might have been. He doesn't get to know." I didn't say anything. There was nothing to say. Cal was a blur in my mind—a face, a smirk, a vague outline of someone I'd never liked and never been able to explain why. Now I had a reason. "I'm not telling him," Leah said. "I'm not telling anyone. This didn't happen." But it had happened. It was still happening, would keep happening long after tonight, in ways neither of us understood yet. The Harpers came back. Mrs Harper's face had changed—not softer, exactly, but different. The panic had been replaced by something tighter, more controlled. She'd been told the facts. She was dealing with them. "Right," she said, her voice too bright. "Let's get you home." She didn't say the word. Neither did Mr Harper. They moved around it, gesturing and touching, offering to carry things and asking about prescriptions. Mr Harper mentioned the car three times in five minutes—*I'll bring the car round, I'll pull up right outside, I'll keep the engine running*—as if the car might fix something. Nobody asked if Leah was okay. Not really. They asked if she was comfortable, if she needed water, if she wanted to sit or stand or lie down. They moved around the word and around the thing itself, careful not to touch either. The discharge papers were signed. The pamphlets were handed over—*Coping with Pregnancy Loss*, *Your Body After Miscarriage*, *Talking to Partners*—and shoved into Mrs Harper's handbag without being looked at. A prescription for iron tablets, because Leah's levels were low. Instructions to rest. Instructions to come back if the bleeding got worse. Standard procedure. The machinery of bad news. We walked to the entrance. The automatic doors slid open and the morning hit us—cold, grey, the particular pale quality of dawn in the north. The car park was half-empty, the night shift drifting away, the day shift not yet arrived. Mr Harper went to get the car. Mrs Harper stood with Leah, one hand on her daughter's shoulder, her face tight. "Thank you," she said to me. "For being there." I nodded. Didn't know what to say. Wasn't sure I'd done anything worth thanking for. The car pulled up. A Range Rover, newish, the kind of car that said we-have-money but we're-not-flashy-about-it. Leah climbed into the back seat without looking at me. "Maya." Mrs Harper again. "Do you need a lift? We can drop you—" "I'm fine. I'll get the bus." It was six AM. The buses had started running again. I could get home, crawl into bed, pretend this night had never happened. That was the plan. That was the only plan. "Alright." Mrs Harper's voice was still too bright. "Well. Thank you. Again." She got into the passenger seat. The car pulled away. Leah didn't look back. I stood in the car park, alone, watching the tail lights disappear into the grey morning. The hospital hummed behind me, constant, indifferent, already dealing with the next crisis, the next night, the next person who couldn't sleep. The sun was coming up.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The writing demonstrates a strong command of the voice established in the planning documents—Maya's sardonic detachment, her physical groundedness, her reluctant decency—and the chapter delivers on its structural promises without overreaching. The strongest element is the handling of the Harpers' arrival. The detail of Mr Harper mentioning the car three times in five minutes is precisely the kind of specific, observed behavior that elevates the writing above generic YA. It's funny and sad simultaneously, which is exactly the tonal register the piece is aiming for. The parents moving "around the word and around the thing itself" is well-executed—the avoidance is shown through action and dialogue rather than announced. The Leah-Cal exchange is the chapter's most emotionally complex moment. "He said he liked that. That I was—clean." The word "clean" landing wrong is genuinely effective. It does a lot of work in very few words: it characterizes Cal without him being present, it deepens Leah's humiliation, and it gives Maya (and the reader) a concrete reason for the vague dislike. This is the chapter at its best—specific, economical, resonant. The dialogue is generally strong. Leah's triple repetition of "I'm fine" on the phone call is a good touch—it reads as authentic teenage performance of composure. The nurse's exchange is brief and functional. Mrs Harper's "Thank you for being there" and Maya's non-response is appropriately understated. Some weaknesses: The observation "The sun was coming up" as a closing line is used here and is also the planned closing of Chapter 8, which suggests either the plan was imprecise or the model is front-loading its ending. It's not a bad line, but it's slightly anticlimactic for a penultimate chapter. The line "But it had happened. It was still happening, would keep happening long after tonight, in ways neither of us understood yet" is the chapter's most significant misstep—it's the kind of authorial intrusion that the plan explicitly warned against, a moment of theme-signaling that tells the reader how to feel rather than trusting them to feel it. It's the one place where the voice slips from Maya's sardonic interiority into something more omniscient and literary.
