The three men passed within ten feet of him. He felt their eyes sweep the wagon, the mare, the driver. He felt them dismiss him. Then one of them stopped. "Wait." Varren pulled the mare to a halt. Turned slowly. "Problem, gentlemen?" The man with the paper stepped closer. He was older than the others, his face hard and weathered. He looked at Varren, then at the wagon bed. "What's under the blanket?" "Cargo. Furs. Delivering to a merchant on Griffin Street." "Let me see." Varren didn't move. "I'm expected. My buyer doesn't like delays." "And the duke doesn't like being kept waiting." The man's hand went to his sword. "Show me what's under the blanket." A choice. Varren had made so many choices today. This one, at least, was easy. He moved before the man could draw—off the wagon, knife in hand, driving the blade into the guard's throat before the sword cleared the scabbard. Blood sprayed hot across his face. The man fell without a sound. The other two were faster. One came at him with a short sword; the other hung back, shouting for help. Varren caught the first blade on his knife, turned it aside, and buried his knife in the man's eye. He fell. The third was running. Calling for the guards. Varren scrambled back to the wagon, grabbed the reins, and drove the mare into the crowd. People scattered. Someone screamed. The gate was ahead—close, so close— "Stop them! Stop them!" The guards at the gate were drawing weapons, but they weren't fast enough. The mare surged through the opening, hooves clattering on stone, and then they were through, on the open road, the city walls falling behind. He didn't stop. He kept the mare at a gallop until the road curved and the city disappeared from view. --- They stopped in a copse of trees, miles from Irongate. The mare was blowing hard, her sides heaving. Varren's hands were shaking. Blood had dried on his face. He could feel it cracking when he moved. Zylara climbed out of the wagon bed, the blanket falling away. She looked at him—really looked—and he couldn't read her expression. "You didn't turn me in," she said. "No." "Why?" He wanted to tell her. He wanted to explain about Pellas, about the money, about the choice that had cracked him open and left something different in its place. But the words were a knot in his chest, and he didn't have the strength to pull them free. "I don't know," he said instead. It was the same thing he'd said to her, that night by the fire. "I don't know what I'm doing anymore." She was quiet for a long moment. Then she reached out and touched his face—her fingers gentle on his jaw, turning him toward her. "Thank you," she said. He pulled away. "Don't. You don't know what this cost me." "I know what it cost you to deliver me. I know what you gave up." Her voice was steady. "I know you just killed three men for me. So don't tell me I don't understand." He looked at her. Her eyes were bright in the fading light, her hair tangled around her horns, her dress stained with travel and something that might have been his blood or her own. She looked wrecked. She looked beautiful. She looked like the end of everything he'd been. "We need to move," he said. "They'll send riders." "Where will we go?" "I don't know." He climbed back onto the wagon and took up the reins. She climbed up beside him. The mare started forward, slow and steady, putting distance between them and the city. Behind them, the sun was setting. Ahead, the road unspooled into darkness. He'd lost the money. He'd lost the future he'd planned. The Syndicate would come for Pellas, and Varren wouldn't be there to stop them. He'd given up everything. And she was still sitting beside him.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is the pivot point of the entire narrative—the moment where Varren makes his choice—and it largely succeeds at executing the plan while carrying some notable weaknesses worth examining carefully. **Strengths:** The chapter follows its plan faithfully. Varren arrives at Irongate, sees the money within reach, and makes the internal choice to turn away before the external conflict forces his hand. The plan specified "no external threats that force the choice," and the chapter mostly honors this: he turns the mare before the duke's men appear. The violence at the gate is a consequence of his choice, not the cause of it. This is structurally sound. The prose is generally controlled and specific. "The city was a fortress built on a hill, its stone grey and imposing" is workmanlike but functional. The chapter avoids the worst excesses of purple prose, though it occasionally reaches for metaphor in ways that don't quite land—"like a wound in the earth" and "like a knife wound" in close proximity is a bit much, and the wound metaphor is overworked throughout. The action sequence is competent and moves with appropriate urgency. The violence is quick and brutal, consistent with Varren's character profile—efficient, no wasted motion. Zylara's behavior is consistent with her arc: she's stopped manipulating, she's waiting, she's trusting him. The reversal of power dynamics is handled without being announced. **Weaknesses:** The chapter's central problem is that Varren's internal decision is underdeveloped. The plan called for a genuinely difficult internal choice from a man with "no code, no hidden nobility." What we get is a montage of memories—Pellas's crooked smile, Zylara's truth-telling—that reads more like a conventional redemption beat than the morally ambiguous rupture the plan promised. The line "He'd never pretended to be anything else, either" is meant to distinguish him from a noble hero, but it doesn't do enough work. The choice feels earned by the story's momentum but not by the specific texture of Varren's psychology. The dialogue is functional but occasionally weak. "Don't. You don't know what this cost me" / "I know what it cost you" is a fairly standard exchange. "I don't know what I'm doing anymore" is the kind of line that sounds meaningful but is actually quite generic. The plan called for "dialogue that fails, misfires, wounds accidentally"—this dialogue is too clean, too articulate about its own emotional content. The ending is the chapter's most significant failure relative to the plan. "She looked like the end of everything he'd been" is exactly the kind of portentous, slightly purple declaration the plan warned against. The final image—"the road unspooled into darkness"—is a cliché. The plan called for an ending that "aches rather than concludes," but this ending concludes quite tidily, with Varren cataloguing his losses and Zylara sitting beside him as a kind of reward. The plan specifically warned against "tidy resolution." The chapter also tells us Varren's emotional state more than it shows it. "He'd lost the money. He'd lost the future he'd planned" is pure tell. The shaking hands are good; the explicit inventory of losses is not. The metaphors are occasionally forced. "The city opened before them like a knife wound—narrow streets bleeding into wider squares" is trying too hard, and the wound/bleeding imagery is already used in the opening paragraph. The repetition suggests a writer reaching for a motif rather than finding one organically. Zylara's "Thank you" and Varren's "Don't" exchange is the chapter's best dialogue moment—brief, specific, and true to both characters. But it's surrounded by more generic emotional articulation. The chapter is competent and largely faithful to its plan, but it softens the edges the plan promised to keep sharp. The choice is made, but it doesn't cost us as readers the way it should. The darkness is present but not fully earned. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 9 Tell-Don't-Show: 8 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 6 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 11 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: The Grave They rode until the moon rose. Varren didn't know where they were going. Away. That was the only plan. Away from Irongate, away from the duke's men, away from the life he'd just set fire to. The road had given way to a track, the track to open country, and still he pushed the mare forward, counting the miles in the rhythm of her hooves. Zylara sat beside him in silence. She'd wrapped the blanket around her shoulders against the cold. Her face was pale in the moonlight, her horns casting thin shadows across her cheeks. She hadn't asked him again why he'd done it. Maybe she understood that he didn't have an answer. Maybe she was just saving her strength. The mare stumbled. Varren pulled her to a stop. They were in a shallow valley between two hills, a stream glinting in the darkness. Good enough. Better than they deserved. "We'll rest here," he said. He climbed down. His legs nearly buckled—the adrenaline had worn off somewhere in the last hour, leaving him shaking and hollow. He'd killed three men. He'd thrown away twelve hundred gold. He'd condemned his brother to whatever mercy the Syndicate chose to show. He untied the mare and let her drink from the stream. Behind him, he heard Zylara climb down from the wagon, her feet landing soft on the grass. "Varren." He didn't turn. "What." "You're bleeding." He looked down. She was right—a cut on his arm, shallow but long, where one of the guards had caught him. He hadn't felt it. He'd been too busy feeling everything else. "It's nothing." "Sit." He should have argued. Should have pushed her away, reminded her that she was still technically his captive, still bound for a king's ransom he'd never collect. But he was tired. So tired. He sat. She knelt beside him. In the moonlight, her face was unreadable. She tore a strip from her already-ruined dress and dipped it in the stream, then pressed the wet cloth to his arm. The cold was a shock. He hissed through his teeth. "Hold still." He held still. Her hands were gentle—too gentle, the kind of touch that came from practice. How many times had she done this for herself? How many wounds had she cleaned in secret, hiding the evidence of her husband's attention? "You don't have to do this," he said. "I know." She kept working. The bleeding slowed, stopped. She bound the wound with another strip of cloth, her fingers deft and certain. When she finished, she didn't move away. She stayed kneeling beside him, close enough to touch, her eyes on his face. "Varren." "What." "Will you take me back tomorrow?" He stared at her. "What?" "The duke's men will search for a day or two. Then they'll assume we've gone to ground. If you turned back now—" "No." "But your brother—" "I said no." She went quiet. The stream rushed over stones. Somewhere in the dark, an owl called. "Why?" Her voice cracked on the word. "You gave up everything. Your money. Your brother. Your life's work. Why would you do that?" He looked at her. This woman who'd manipulated him, wounded him, forced him to see things he'd spent eleven years avoiding. This woman who'd told him the ugliest truth about herself and waited for him to look away. He hadn't looked away. "I don't know," he said. She made a sound that might have been a laugh. "You keep saying that." "It's the only honest answer I have." She was trembling. He could see it now—the shake in her shoulders, the way her hands pressed against her thighs. She'd been holding herself together since they'd fled the city. Longer than that. Years longer. "Come here," he said. She hesitated. Then she moved—crawling the small distance between them, collapsing against his chest. He wrapped his arms around her and held on. She was warm. She was alive. She was the reason he'd thrown everything away, and he couldn't bring himself to regret it. "I'm terrified," she whispered against his shoulder. "I've been terrified for days. And I couldn't—" "I know." "I've been alone for so long. Even when I was manipulating you. Even when I was using my body to—" "I know." She pulled back to look at him. Her eyes were wet. "Do you? Do you actually know?" He thought about eleven years of contracts. The weight of the rope in his hands. The faces he'd stopped seeing. The counting he did to keep from thinking. "I know," he said. "I've been alone too." --- They lay in the back of the wagon, side by side, staring up at the stars. He'd started a small fire, more for light than warmth, and it crackled and spat in the darkness. His arm ached where she'd bandaged it. His whole body ached—from the fight, from the riding, from the weight of what he'd done. Beside him, Zylara was breathing slowly. Not asleep—he could tell by the rhythm—but resting. Letting her guard down in a way she hadn't since he'd first tied her hands. "Varren?" "What." "What do we do now?" He considered the question. The Syndicate would hunt him. The duke would hunt them both. Pellas was on his own, and Varren couldn't think about that without feeling sick. "I don't know," he said. "Keep moving. Find somewhere to hide. Figure it out as we go." "That's not a plan." "No. It's not." She was quiet for a moment. Then: "I've never had a plan. Not really. I was sold at fifteen, married at sixteen, widowed at twenty-two. Everything that happened to me was someone else's decision." She turned her head to look at him. "This is the first time I've chosen. And I don't know what comes next." He turned to look at her. In the firelight, her face was soft. Young, almost. She'd looked like this the night she'd told him about Corvin—the walls down, the truth bleeding through. "What did you choose?" he asked. "To trust you. To tell you things I've never told anyone." A pause. "To stay." "You're free," he said. "You know that. I'm not—you can go. I won't stop you." She smiled. It was small and sad and real. "Where would I go? My family sold me. My husband is dead. The duke wants me for his vengeance." She shook her head. "You're the first person in years who's looked at me like I'm a person and not a prize. You're the first person I've told the truth to." "That doesn't mean you owe me—" "I don't owe you anything." She said it gently, without heat. "But I want to stay. If you'll have me." He didn't know how to answer that. He'd never known how to answer anything that mattered. "I don't know if I can give you anything," he said finally. "I'm not a good man. I've done things—" "So have I." "I hurt you. I took what I wanted and I didn't ask." "Yes." She held his gaze. "And I let you. And some part of me wanted it too. Does that make us even? Or just broken in the same way?" He didn't have an answer. He suspected there wasn't one. "I'm frightened," he said. It was the first time he'd admitted it—to himself, to anyone. "I don't know what happens next. I don't know if we'll survive this." "Neither do I." She reached out and took his hand. Her fingers intertwined with his, rough bandages and callused skin. "But I'd rather be frightened with you than alone." --- They didn't sleep. Not really. They lay in the wagon, side by side, her head on his shoulder, his hand in her hair. The fire burned low. The stars wheeled overhead. Somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled, and another answered. He thought about Pellas. About the money he'd never deliver, the debt that would never be paid. About his brother's face when the Syndicate's men came back, and there was no one left to save him. He thought about the three men he'd killed. Not the first men he'd killed—probably not the last—but the first he'd killed for something other than money. He thought about Zylara. About the way she'd looked at him when he'd turned the wagon away from the keep. About the sound of her voice when she'd said *I want to stay*. He didn't know what they were. Captor and captive, yes—but the rope was gone now, and she was still here. Enemies, maybe—but enemies didn't hold each other in the dark. Something else. Something he didn't have a word for. "Varren?" Her voice was drowsy, half-asleep. "What." "Thank you. For not turning me in." He was quiet for a long time. The fire was almost out. The moon had set. In a few hours, the sun would rise, and they'd have to move again—run again—fight again. "Don't thank me," he said finally. "I didn't do it for you." She made a soft sound. Almost a laugh. "I know. But I'm thanking you anyway." He closed his eyes. In the morning, there would be consequences. The duke's riders. The Syndicate's collectors. The weight of a choice that couldn't be undone. But for now, in the dark, there was just this: her breathing, slow and steady against his chest. The warmth of her body. The strange, terrifying possibility that he might not have to be alone anymore. He didn't sleep. But for the first time in years, the silence in his head was quiet. The sun would come. The road would wait. Whatever they were becoming, they'd find out together.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the concluding chapter of this story, and it carries the weight of everything that came before. Let me assess it carefully against the plan, the character profiles, and the writing prompt. **Adherence to Chapter Plan:** The plan called for: Varren wounded, positions reversed with Zylara tending him, their reversed dynamic, her asking if he'll take her back tomorrow, him not answering, her watching him feign sleep, knowing the choice has cost him everything, an open and frightening future. The chapter follows this reasonably well but diverges in significant ways. The plan specified "he doesn't answer" to her question about taking her back—but in the chapter, he answers immediately and emphatically ("No"). The plan called for him to feign sleep while she watches, maintaining ambiguity and terror. Instead, the chapter ends with him awake, holding her, in a relatively warm emotional resolution. The plan explicitly warned against "tidy resolution" and called for an ending that "aches rather than concludes." The chapter largely fails this—it concludes with warmth and tentative hope rather than the frightening openness the plan demanded. **Following the Writing Prompt's Spirit:** The prompt established a gritty, morally compromised dynamic. The chapter softens this considerably. The plan's intention section explicitly warned against "redemption arcs" and "tidy resolution," yet the chapter delivers something close to both. **Character Consistency:** Varren's voice profile emphasizes that he "has never apologized to anyone for anything, not once" and that he avoids moral questions by refusing to ask them. Yet in this chapter he says "I hurt you. I took what I wanted and I didn't ask"—which is essentially an apology and a moral reckoning, delivered cleanly and without the resistance the character profile demands. This feels inconsistent with the established character. Zylara's profile notes she "doesn't trust kindness, assumes it's manipulation she hasn't decoded yet" and is "terrified of being seen clearly, but also desperately wants someone to see her." The chapter captures the second half of this but not the first—she accepts warmth too readily, without the suspicion her profile demands. **Dialogue Quality:** The dialogue is functional but frequently falls into the trap the plan warned against: it's too articulate, too emotionally legible. Lines like "You're the first person in years who's looked at me like I'm a person and not a prize" are on-the-nose exposition of theme rather than character speaking. "I'd rather be frightened with you than alone" is a romance novel line, not the voice of a woman who manipulates instinctively and wounds deliberately. The plan called for "dialogue that fails, misfires, wounds accidentally"—instead the dialogue here is emotionally successful and thematically tidy. **Show vs. Tell:** There's significant telling. "She'd been holding herself together since they'd fled the city. Longer than that. Years longer." This is the narrator explaining emotional backstory rather than dramatizing it. "He'd never known how to answer anything that mattered" is similarly explanatory. The chapter tells us about their loneliness rather than showing it through behavior. **Prose Quality:** The prose is competent but not distinctive. Some lines work: "the adrenaline had worn off somewhere in the last hour, leaving him shaking and hollow" is good. "The fire burned low. The stars wheeled overhead" is serviceable. But there's also generic fantasy-romance prose: "her face was soft. Young, almost." The ending—"the silence in his head was quiet"—is a mixed metaphor (silence being quiet is redundant) that tries for profundity and misses. **Emotional Engagement:** Despite its flaws, the chapter does generate some emotional warmth. The wound-tending scene has physical specificity. The reversal of their dynamic (her tending him) is effective. But the emotional resolution feels unearned given the plan's insistence on moral complexity—we're told they're broken in the same way, but the chapter doesn't dramatize that brokenness; it resolves it. **Creativity and Surprise:** The chapter is largely predictable. Two damaged people find tentative connection in the dark—this is a familiar romance arc. The plan promised something more unsettling and open-ended. The "I'd rather be frightened with you than alone" line is particularly generic. **Well-earned Darkness/Lightness:** The lightness here is not well-earned. The plan explicitly called for an ending where "neither can name" what they are, where the future is "open and frightening," where they're "not even sure they like each other." The chapter delivers something warmer and more resolved than this. Varren's admission "I've been alone too" and the hand-holding feel like they belong in a different, gentler story. **Overall Assessment:** This is a competent but significantly compromised final chapter. It abandons the plan's most interesting and difficult demands—the ambiguity, the unresolved terror, the characters who wound each other—in favor of a warmer, more conventional romantic resolution. The prose is adequate but not distinctive. The dialogue is too articulate and emotionally successful. The characters are softer versions of themselves. It reads like a writer who understood the plan intellectually but defaulted to comfort when executing it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 9 Emotionally Engaging: 11 Compelling Plot: 8 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 12 Tell-Don't-Show: 13 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 14 Amateurish: 9 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 7 Characters Consistent with Profile: 8 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 10

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a competent, readable piece of fantasy erotica that demonstrates genuine craft in several areas while also exhibiting notable weaknesses that prevent it from reaching its potential. STRENGTHS: The characterization of Varren and Zylara is the story's strongest element. Both characters have internal consistency: Varren's professional detachment masking guilt and resentment, Zylara's calculated manipulation gradually giving way to genuine vulnerability. The detail of Varren counting jolts to avoid thinking is well-deployed as a recurring motif. Zylara's backstory with Corvin is genuinely dark and handled with restraint—the flashback in Chapter 2 is the story's best writing, specific and visceral without being gratuitous. The dialogue is generally strong. Lines like "You're the one holding the rope. Tell me—which is worse?" and the exchange about Pellas's debt feel earned and character-specific. The characters speak distinctly. WEAKNESSES: The transformation in Chapter 5 (the sexual encounter) is the story's most significant failure. The scene is rushed and tonally confused—described as "more like combat than coupling" but then immediately pivoting to emotional vulnerability. The roughness is framed ambiguously in a way that feels evasive rather than intentional. More critically, the aftermath is handled poorly: Varren retying her hands tighter after sex reads as punishment, which the narrative acknowledges but doesn't fully reckon with. The story wants this to be a complicated, morally fraught moment but doesn't commit to examining what it means. The ending is the story's second major weakness. Varren's decision to turn away from Irongate—abandoning twelve hundred gold, condemning his brother, killing three men—happens too quickly and with insufficient internal justification. The narrative tells us he's decided but doesn't show the actual moment of decision with enough weight. "He closed his eyes. Then he opened them, turned the mare away from the keep" is doing enormous work that it hasn't earned. This is the central unearned transformation. The prose occasionally tips into purple territory: "The city opened before them like a knife wound" is a strained metaphor. "She looked like the end of everything he'd been" is overwrought. The recurring "I don't know" as a character tic becomes repetitive to the point of self-parody by Chapter 8, where it appears four or five times in quick succession. The pacing is uneven. Chapters 1-3 are well-paced, building tension effectively. Chapter 4 (the storm/kiss) rushes the emotional pivot. Chapter 5 is the weakest structurally. Chapters 6-8 recover somewhat but feel rushed toward resolution. The world-building is thin—Irongate, the Thornback Syndicate, the duke are sketched but not developed. This is acceptable for a short story but occasionally the lack of specificity weakens stakes. The "I don't know" repetition, the wolf howling at the end, the stars wheeling overhead—these are familiar genre gestures deployed without fresh perspective. The story is competent but rarely surprising. The sexual content is present but handled awkwardly—the story seems uncertain whether to lean into the erotica elements or treat them as character beats, and the result is neither fully satisfying as erotica nor as literary fiction. The prompt is faithfully followed: tiefling captive, bounty hunter, three days from Irongate, the "talk him into an early grave" dynamic is well-realized. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 12 Nuanced Characters: 14 Pacing: 11 World Building: 9 Compelling Plot: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 10 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 8 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Unearned Transformations: 12 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16
Avg Final Item Score: 59.6/100
+ Cozy-Horror Post Apocalyptic Survival — Mushroom Season (Chapter Avg: 78.4 | Final: 64.3)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you're out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious?
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you&#x27;re out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious? -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Characters **Protagonist Options:** - A former line cook or home cook enthusiast who finds purpose again through "monster cuisine" - A grandmotherly figure with deep cooking knowledge and survivor wisdom - A young person who never learned to cook, discovering both food and identity - A former chef at a high-end restaurant, now reduced to survival cooking - A pair of siblings - one cooks, one hunts - A lone survivor who reluctantly takes in others **Supporting Cast Possibilities:** - A reluctant companion who's horrified by eating "people" (if mutants were human) - A child who innocently accepts the food - An older mentor figure who remembers "before" - A scientist or mycologist who understands the spores - A hunter/guard who provides the ingredients - A community of survivors skeptical but hungry **Antagonist Forces:** - The spore mutants themselves (various types, dangers) - Other desperate survivors - Internal conflict about what they're eating - A particular alpha mutant or hive mind - Environmental hazards (weather, radiation, etc.) - Someone who wants to control the food supply ## Plot Directions **Discovery Scenarios:** - Desperation-driven accident (killing one, being hungry enough to try) - Watching animals eat the remains and survive - An old survival manual with controversial advice - A dying survivor's confession/secret - Burning a body and smelling "pork" - A child's innocent experimentation **Conflict Threads:** - Is it cannibalism if they're not human anymore? - Do the mutants retain memories or consciousness? - Does eating them change you over time? - What happens when other survivors want in? - The mutants becoming harder to hunt / adapting - Finding out the "truth" of what they are **Possible Arcs:** 1. **Culinary Journey**: From desperate survival to "gourmet" monster cooking, building a reputation 2. **Found Family**: Feeding others, creating community through shared meals 3. **Dark Secret**: Hiding what the food is, eventual reveal and consequences 4. **Transformation**: Protagonist changes (physically/mentally) from consumption 5. **Hunting Party**: Group dynamics around dangerous food procurement 6. **Ethical Dilemma**: Discovery that mutants retain humanity leads to moral crisis ## Tone Approaches **Cozy Elements to Weave:** - Detailed, sensory cooking scenes (warmth, smell, taste) - Candlelit meals, shared bread, conversation - Creating home in ruined spaces - Small pleasures: tea, preserved spices, a good knife - Humor around the absurdity of "gourmet apocalypse" - Comfort of routine (hunting, preparing, eating) - Beauty in decay (flowers growing through concrete, etc.) **Horror Elements to Balance:** - What the mutants look like up close - The violence of killing - Sounds in the night, movement in the shadows - Memories of who was lost to the spores - The gnawing question: are we eating people? - Physical danger during hunts - Body horror elements of the mutants themselves **Cozy-Horror Blend Approaches:** - Domestic routine with monstrous ingredients - Warm kitchen scenes where the "meat" has unsettling qualities - Community love built on an unsettling foundation - Beautiful meals with quiet horror implications - Hope and dread intertwined ## Setting Options - Rural farmhouse with root cellar and woodstove - Suburban neighborhood, fortified - Small town main street with several secured buildings - An abandoned restaurant or inn - Urban apartment with rooftop garden access - Underground bunker with greenhouse ## Story Beats for 8 Chapters **Potential Chapter Breakdowns:** **Version A - Discovery to Community:** 1. Desperate hunger, first kill, accidental discovery 2. Cautious experimentation, first intentional meal 3. Finding another survivor, sharing food 4. Building trust, teaching cooking, growing group 5. A dangerous hunt, raising stakes 6. Community meal, deepening bonds 7. Revelation or crisis (what are we eating?) 8. Acceptance and sustainable future **Version B - Secret and Revelation:** 1. Starving protagonist, discovery of edibility 2. Learning to cook them, finding satisfaction 3. Taking in others, hiding the source 4. Growing community, protagonist as provider 5. Someone grows suspicious or discovers truth 6. Confrontation, splintering of group 7. Night of crisis (attack, confession, or both) 8. New understanding, moving forward together **Version C - Culinary Adventure:** 1. The last can of beans, the desperate hunt 2. The discovery - fear, revulsion, hunger winning 3. First recipe development, strange ingredients 4. Others arrive, dinners become gatherings 5. Hunting as a team, different "cuts" of mutant 6. A feast that draws unwanted attention 7. Defense of the home, losses and gains 8. New normal, recipes for the future ## The Spore Mutants - What Are They? **Possibilities:** - Former humans transformed by fungal infection - Alien or interdimensional creatures that arrived with spores - Plants/animals mutated by the spores - Grown from the earth itself, "harvested" beings - A mix - some were human, some weren't **Sensory Details:** - Texture of meat: varies by type (some like pork, some like fish, some like gamey venison) - Appearance: mushroom-like growths, bioluminescence, strange coloration - Sound: rustling, spore-release hissing, gurgling - Smell: earthy, fungal, changes when cooked - Taste: umami-rich, complex, satisfying ## Thematic Angles - **Sustenance and Survival**: What we'll do to stay alive - **Community and Food**: How meals bind us - **The Monstrous and the Mundane**: Normalizing horror - **Adaptation and Acceptance**: Moving through revulsion to necessity - **Memory and Loss**: Those we've lost vs. what we eat - **Cycles and Sustainability**: Nature's food chain, even if strange - **Domesticity in Extremis**: Making home in apocalypse --- # Reflection Looking at these ideas, I'm noticing several threads that feel particularly strong for the "cozy-horror" prompt. **What Works Best:** The **former line cook protagonist** feels right. They have skills that matter, a framework for processing the world (through food), and a sense of loss (their profession, their kitchen, their purpose) that makes the discovery of cooking mutants feel like a reclaiming of identity. It also gives natural language for describing food in compelling, sensory ways. The **found family through shared meals** thread is perfect for cozy-horror. Food is inherently communal and nurturing. The horror of *what* they're eating contrasted with the warmth of *how* they're eating it creates that specific tension the genre thrives on. A candlelit dinner where everyone is grateful and laughing, while the "pork roast" on the table used to be a monster trying to kill them—or worse, a person—is exactly the needle we want to thread. For setting, a **farmhouse or small-town restaurant** gives us the domestic space we need for cozy scenes (kitchen, dining room, fireplace) while also providing hunting grounds and a reason for travelers to arrive. It's defensible but not militaristic. **The mutants being former humans** creates the richest horror element. The revulsion, the ethical questions, the grief—it adds weight to every meal. But I don't want to definitively answer whether they retain humanity. The *not knowing* is scarier. Let readers sit in that discomfort while characters find their own peace with it. **What Doesn't Work as Well:** A pure **secret-and-reveal plot** feels too neat. If the horror is that they're eating former people, having a dramatic reveal in chapter 6-7 makes it too much the *point* of the story. Better if the horror is always present, always questioned, and the story is about living with that knowledge, not discovering it. **Pure survival action** would lose the cozy. We need the cooking scenes, the conversations, the warmth. The story should spend as much time in the kitchen as in the field. **A large cast** would diffuse the intimacy. 3-5 characters maximum. The protagonist, maybe one or two others they take in, perhaps a child for stakes. That's enough for community without losing focus. **Pacing Considerations:** Eight chapters at ~1000 words each means we need efficiency. Each chapter should have: - One main event/emotion - At least one sensory-rich cooking or eating scene - A balance of cozy and horror elements - Forward momentum toward community-building **The Story I Want to Tell:** A former cook, alone and starving, discovers that the spore mutants are edible. Through cooking them, she reclaims her purpose. She takes in other survivors, creating a strange little family bound by these shared, terrible, delicious meals. The horror is always present—in what they eat, in the hunts, in the loss that brought them here—but so is the warmth. By the end, they've built something sustainable: a home, a routine, a way forward. Not a solution to the apocalypse, but a way to live within it with some joy. The tension I'm most excited about is this: **the meals are genuinely nurturing, and the food is genuinely good.** That's the cozy. The horror is what the food *is*. Neither cancels the other out. They coexist, and the characters learn to coexist with that contradiction.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Protagonist:** Marta, a former line cook in her late 30s who worked at a decent farm-to-table restaurant before the world ended. She's not a chef, not famous—just someone who loved her work and lost everything when the spores came. She's been surviving alone for months, her skills useless until now. **Setting:** An abandoned farmhouse on the edge of a small town, partially fortified. It has a working woodstove, a root cellar (mostly empty), and a large kitchen. Rural enough to have space, close enough to town for scavenging and hunting. **Supporting Cast:** - **Dylan**, 19, who Marta finds half-starved in Chapter 3. He was too young when the world ended to have many skills. He becomes her helper, her hunter-in-training. - **Cass**, mid-40s, a pragmatist who arrives in Chapter 4 with practical skills and hard-won survival knowledge. She provides balance—someone who questions but accepts. - Possibly a **child** (Dylan's younger sister, ~8 years old, named **Lily**) to raise the stakes and soften the horror. A child who eats without asking questions. **Plot Arc:** Marta discovers the mutants are edible through desperation. She begins cooking them, reclaiming her identity through food. Survivors arrive; she feeds them. A community forms around her table. The horror is never hidden from the reader—we know what they're eating—but the characters move through their revulsion to acceptance. The climax involves a dangerous hunt that threatens what they've built, and the resolution is a quiet one: they continue. They've found a way to live. **Voice:** First person from Marta's perspective. Intimate, sensory, wry. She has a cook's vocabulary for describing food, which she applies to monstrous ingredients without flinching. Her narration should make the reader hungry and uncomfortable simultaneously. **Tone Balance:** Each chapter will contain at least one extended cooking or eating scene, rendered warmly and in detail. Horror lives in the hunts, in the appearance of the mutants, in the quiet questions about what they once were. The horror is present but not overwhelming. Cozy is the bass note; horror is the discordant harmony. **What I Will Avoid:** - A dramatic reveal that they're eating people—readers know from the premise - Action-heavy survival at the expense of domestic scenes - Definitive answers about whether mutants retain humanity - Hopeless endings or complete tragedy - Large communities that diffuse intimacy --- # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: "Hunger" Marta is down to her last can of peaches. She's weak, light-headed, has been for weeks. A spore mutant—a "shambler," riddled with fungal growths, lumbers onto her property. She kills it out of habit and defense, not thinking of food. But in the aftermath, she's so hungry she considers it. The smell when she burns the body to dispose of it triggers something primal. She cuts a piece, cooks it thoroughly, and eats with trembling hands. The taste is rich, complex, satisfying. Her first thought: *that would pair well with rosemary.* The chapter ends with her full for the first time in months, staring at what remains of the creature, understanding what she's done. **Key beats:** Physical desperation, the kill, the smell, the first bite, the dark humor of her culinary instincts surfacing. --- ## Chapter 2: "Preparation" Marta processes what she's eating. She butchers the remaining meat, discovers different cuts with different textures, starts thinking like a cook again. She finds wild herbs, salt from a distant gas station, a few surviving spices. Her first intentional meal is almost religious—the care she takes, the plating, the ritual of eating at a table with a cloth napkin. She feels alive for the first time since the world ended. But she also examines the creature's remains more closely, seeing fragments of what might have been human clothing, a glint of something metal. She chooses not to look too closely. She preserves what she can, smokes meat, fills her root cellar. Purpose returns. **Key beats:** Butchery as craft, the reclaiming of identity, first real meal, the choice not to investigate too deeply. --- ## Chapter 3: "Guest" Dylan appears, half-starved, trying to steal from her root cellar. She catches him, feeds him. He eats ravenously without asking what the meat is. When he's full, he thanks her, asks if there's more. He's been alone for months. She lets him stay. He's young, lost, grateful. She doesn't tell him what he's eaten—not yet. Or maybe she does, and his reaction is simply: "They were going to kill me anyway. Might as well make myself useful after." They establish a dynamic: he'll help with the heavy work, the hunting. She'll cook. A partnership forms. **Key beats:** Discovery of Dylan, the first meal shared, the beginning of community, the question of disclosure. --- ## Chapter 4: "Table" Cass arrives, cautious, armed. She's a survivor who's seen things. She's suspicious of Marta's food supply, asks direct questions. Marta shows her a shambler in the woodpile, explains. Cass is horrified—but hungry. She eats. They all eat. The dinner conversation is tense then slowly warms. They talk about before, about what they've lost. Cass reveals she was a nurse. She's seen what the spores do to people. She knows, better than they do, what these creatures might have been. She says: "It's not them anymore. The person is gone. This is just... what's left." It's not comfort, exactly, but it's permission. We learn about Lily—Dylan's younger sister, whom he's been protecting, who's been hiding in the barn, too scared to come out. **Key beats:** Cass's arrival, the confrontation, the meal that breaks tension, the ethical framework offered, introduction of Lily. --- ## Chapter 5: "Hunt" The freezer is getting low. Marta, Dylan, and Cass go on a hunt together. We see the creatures in their element—shambling through an abandoned downtown, the fungal networks pulsing, the way they move in groups. It's dangerous, tense work. They track a small cluster, set up an ambush. A close call when one gets too close to Lily (who insisted on coming). Dylan makes his first kill. Marta finishes another. They field-dress the creatures, bring home the meat. The horror of the hunt contrasts with the domestic warmth of the kitchen after. Lily asks if she can help cook. **Key beats:** The hunt as action, the mutants as threat, Lily in danger, Dylan's first kill, return to warmth. --- ## Chapter 6: "Feast" It's Lily's birthday. Marta decides to make something special—a proper celebration, the kind that wouldn't have been remarkable before but now means everything. She prepares an elaborate meal, uses her best preserved ingredients, plates it beautifully. They eat by candlelight. Dylan produces a small gift he'd been saving—something he found scavenging. Cass tells stories. Lily laughs. For an evening, the world feels almost normal. The horror is beneath the surface but not erased: Marta watches Lily eat with appetite, watches Dylan scrape his plate, and feels both profound love and a quiet, persistent unease. Is this right? Is she corrupting them? But they're alive. They're happy. They're full. **Key beats:** Domestic celebration, joy in apocalypse, the contrast of warmth and underlying dread, love as justification. --- ## Chapter 7: "Fallow" A hard winter. The mutants are hibernating or migrating—scarce. Food runs low again. Tensions rise. Dylan suggests going further afield, taking risks. Cass argues for rationing. Marta tries to keep peace. They're hungry, irritable, scared. A hunt goes badly—Dylan is injured, Cass barely escapes. They return with less meat than they need. Marta stares at her empty root cellar, her dwindling supplies, and feels the old desperation returning. She wonders if this is sustainable, if they've just delayed the inevitable. But then, the thaw begins. A mutant stumbles onto the property, drawn by something. It's a reminder that the world hasn't stopped providing. But Marta also notices something: the creature has a tattoo on its arm. A name. Someone's daughter. She butchers it anyway. **Key beats:** Scarcity and tension, failed hunt, the return of desperation, the thaw, the tattoo, the choice renewed. --- ## Chapter 8: "Spring" The world is greening. They've made it through winter. Marta plants a garden—real vegetables, herbs, things that will take months to yield. Cass helps. Dylan teaches Lily to hunt. They're building something. A new mutant comes, and they handle it with practiced efficiency. The horror has become routine, and in that routine, there's comfort. They eat dinner together. Marta thinks about the tattoo, about the name she saw. She doesn't share it. Some things are hers to carry. The story ends with them at the table—full, warm, together. Outside, the world is broken, but inside, there's soup. There's bread. There's family. It's enough. **Key beats:** Renewal, sustainability, the future, private grief, acceptance, the final meal as affirmation.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
## How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human writer would approach this premise with **personal, specific obsession**. They'd have a reason they're drawn to it that isn't just "it's an interesting concept." Maybe they've experienced real hunger. Maybe they worked in restaurants and know the particular intimacy of handling meat. Maybe they're disturbed by their own curiosity about cannibalism and are writing to process that. A human brings **baggage** to a story like this—and that's what makes it feel alive. A human would also approach the cooking scenes with **lived sensory knowledge**. They'd know that butchery is messy—blood under the fingernails, the surprising resistance of connective tissue, the way your lower back aches from standing. They'd know hunger isn't noble; it's ugly. It makes you irritable, stupid, willing to do things you'd never imagined. A human would let their character be petty and desperate and not always sympathetic. Perhaps most importantly, a human wouldn't **resolve** the ethical tension. They might not even know what they think about it. The story would hold the question without answering it. Is this wrong? Are they still human? Should we feel guilty? A human writer might land somewhere different on page one versus page eight thousand—and that shifting relationship would be visible on the page. --- ## Telltale LLM Approaches (And Why They Fail This Piece) **The Neat Emotional Bow** LLMs tend to land on resolution. Characters process their feelings, come to terms with things, find peace. The ending says "it was enough." But cozy-horror lives in **sustained dissonance**—the comfort and the horror existing simultaneously, neither resolving. A neat conclusion undermines the entire premise. **Functional Character Design** "Protagonist is a cook, Cass is a nurse, Dylan is a hunter-in-training." This is role assignment, not character creation. Humans write people who have jobs, not jobs that happen to be people. A real person has irrelevant details, skills that don't serve the plot, contradictions that never pay off. **Thesis-Driven Theme** LLMs often write as if proving a point: "This story explores what it means to be human..." A human writer explores because they **don't know the answer**. They're thinking on the page. The theme emerges organically, often in ways the writer didn't intend. **Poetic But Empty Language** "The darkness pressed against the windows like a living thing." This sounds impressive but means nothing. It's decorative. A human writes specifically: "The window had been boarded wrong; there was a gap at the corner where the cold came through." **Emotional Explaining** "She felt a mix of horror and hunger, a paradox that gnawed at her soul." This tells the reader what to feel instead of making them feel it. A human would describe the hands shaking, the mouth watering, the self-loathing—and let the contradiction exist without naming it. **Safe Transgression** LLMs often transgress in ways that are ultimately safe. The characters eat monsters, but we're reassured they're good people in a bad situation. A human might make them less sympathetic—might let them enjoy it too much, might let them stop asking questions. **Symmetrical Structure** Each chapter getting "one cooking scene, one horror beat" is too mechanical. Real stories breathe differently—some chapters heavy on domesticity, some on dread, some on character. The rhythm should serve the emotional truth, not a structural template. **Characters Who Speak in Complete Thoughts** Real dialogue is messy. People interrupt, trail off, reference things the listener doesn't understand. LLMs tend to write dialogue that communicates too efficiently. --- ## Deep Critique of the Current Plan ### The "Found Family" Trope Is Too Easy This is the most significant problem. The plan relies on the warmest possible version of apocalypse survival—strangers becoming a loving family around the dinner table. It's **comfortable**. It lets the reader feel good about what they're reading while the horror stays safely external (the monsters outside). A human would complicate this. Found families have friction. They have: - Unequal power dynamics (Marta controls the food) - Unspoken resentments (Dylan might feel like a servant, Cass might feel like an outsider) - Incompatible coping mechanisms (Dylan's dark humor vs. Cass's pragmatism vs. Marta's denial) - Dependencies that aren't purely loving (where would Dylan go? He *has* to stay) The plan has them "building trust" and "deepening bonds" but doesn't show the **work**—the arguments, the silences, the times someone leaves the table angry. ### Marta Is Too Functional Her entire character serves the premise: she's a cook who cooks monsters. That's it. But a human would ask: - What did she lose besides her job? - What's her specific damage? - Does she have bad habits? Pet peeves? Weird beliefs? - What does she do when she's *not* cooking? - Does she even *like* these people, or does she just need them? The plan says she "reclaims her identity through cooking." That's redemptive and neat. What if the opposite is true? What if cooking used to be her joy, and now it's tainted? What if she's good at it but hates herself for being good at it? ### The Tattoo in Chapter 7 Is Theatrical This is a classic LLM move—inserting a dramatic symbol at a calculated moment. "A tattoo. A name. Someone's daughter." It's designed to create a specific emotional effect. A human would handle the reminder of humanity differently: - It might happen gradually, in small ways, not as a climactic reveal - It might be something mundane and therefore more disturbing (a watch still ticking, a grocery list in a pocket) - It might not lead to any internal crisis—Marta might notice it and just... keep working. The horror of that non-reaction would be more powerful than a chapter's worth of anguish. ### Cass Provides Too Much Moral Clarity A nurse who says "The person is gone. This is just what's left" is functioning as the story's ethical framework. She's giving permission, resolving doubt. This is the LLM tendency toward safety—making sure the reader knows the characters are still good. A human would make Cass **less helpful**. Maybe Cass was a nurse and couldn't save anyone. Maybe she's the most disturbed by the eating because she knows too much about bodies. Maybe she has no framework at all—just silence when the topic comes up. A character who *should* have answers but *doesn't* is far more human. ### Lily Is "Innocence" As Plot Function Making her eight, making her the birthday girl, making her ask to help cook—these are engineered to create warmth. She's a symbol, not a person. A human would write a real child. Children in trauma aren't just sweet. They're: - Weird (Lily might collect things from the dead mutants) - Honest in uncomfortable ways ("Was this one a mom?") - Resilient in ways that seem wrong (she might not be scared of the mutants at all) - Prone to behavior problems, not just gratitude ### The Ending Resolves Too Much "It's enough." The garden planted, the family made, the future possible. This is an LLM landing—emotionally complete, thematically tidy. But cozy-horror works best when it **doesn't resolve**. A human ending might: - Show Marta still hungry after a meal - End on a detail that's both warm and wrong (Lily playing with a button she found on a mutant) - Raise a new question rather than answering old ones - Be comfortable in an uncomfortable way—showing adaptation that's also moral erosion ### The Cooking Scenes Risk Self-Parody "warm, sensory, detailed" cooking scenes described in culinary language could become ridiculous. A human who's actually cooked would know: - Cooking is physically demanding. Your feet hurt. Your hands dry out. - Things go wrong. Food burns. Seasoning is off. - Good food doesn't always look beautiful. - The satisfaction is real but so is the tedium. The plan has Marta plating beautifully, using cloth napkins, creating "almost religious" meals. This is aestheticized survival. A human might let the meals be uglier, more practical—and therefore more real. ### The Horror Is Too Distributed One horror beat per chapter is formulaic. It turns dread into a schedule. A human would let horror be **unpredictable**: - Some chapters would have no mutants at all, just the fear of them - Some would have too many, overwhelming - The horror might come from characters' reactions, not the creatures themselves - It might build slowly across chapters, or disappear entirely when the characters need a break ### The Ethical Question Is Too Present The plan has characters directly confronting "what are we eating?" multiple times. This is the LLM tendency to **make the theme explicit**. A human might let it go unspoken for long stretches. People in survival situations don't constantly interrogate their choices—they get on with it. The horror of not asking can be more powerful than the drama of asking. --- ## Brainstorming Solutions ### Character Fixes **Marta:** - Give her a specific, non-functional quirk. She saves seeds even though she's bad at gardening. She alphabetizes her spices even though half are empty. She has a favorite mug she won't let anyone else use. - Make her relationship to cooking complicated. Maybe she resents that this is her value now. Maybe she feels like a fraud because she never went to culinary school. Maybe she loved cooking before and now it's ruined. - Give her a loss that haunts her specifically. Someone she couldn't save. A choice she made that she can't justify. - Let her be wrong about things. Not morally wrong—just practically wrong. She makes mistakes. **Dylan:** - He's bad at hunting. Genuinely bad. He practices and improves but it's slow and embarrassing. - He has his own damage that isn't about Lily. A friend he abandoned. A place he should have stayed. - He and Lily fight like real siblings. They love each other and also she's annoying and he needs space from her. - He doesn't just "accept" the food. He asks questions that make Marta uncomfortable. Or he refuses to ask them, which makes Cass uncomfortable. **Cass:** - Remove "nurse" as identity. She was something else entirely—an accountant, a Lyft driver, a preschool teacher. Something that gave her no useful skills. She's learning from scratch. - OR: She was a nurse, but she's the most disturbed by the bodies because she can't stop seeing patients. - She doesn't fit in. She rubs people wrong. She makes dark jokes that aren't funny. She's not hostile, just awkward. - She might leave. Or threaten to. The family isn't guaranteed. **Lily:** - Give her a specific, slightly wrong coping mechanism. She collects things from the mutants. She gives them names. She wants to know exactly how they're killed. - She has bad dreams but won't talk about them. - She adores Dylan but also resents him for the things he can't protect her from. - She asks one question per meal that no one wants to answer. ### Structural Fixes **Asymmetry:** - Chapter 3 shouldn't just be "Dylan arrives, eats, accepts." Maybe he arrives, eats, gets sick (unrelated), Marta nurses him, they fight, he tries to leave, comes back. Messier. - Chapter 5 and 7 both have hunts. Make one a hunt and one something else—scavenging, or a human threat, or a weather crisis. - Chapter 6 birthday party is too neat. Make it awkward. Dylan forgot. The food is slightly wrong. Someone cries for no clear reason. **Pacing:** - Let some chapters breathe with no horror at all, just domestic life. Then hit hard when the horror returns. - The mutants shouldn't appear on schedule. They should feel like a natural phenomenon with patterns the characters don't fully understand. ### Ending Fixes **Reject resolution.** Instead of "it's enough": - End with Marta catching herself wondering what a specific mutant tasted like, and being disturbed by her own curiosity. - End with Lily showing Marta something she found—a photo in a mutant's pocket—and Marta realizing she doesn't want to look at it. - End with them at the table, and the moment is warm, but Marta's hand shakes slightly. Just slightly. And she doesn't know why. The ending should leave the reader with **sustained discomfort** that coexists with the warmth. Not answers. Not peace. Just a table, and food, and people who need each other, and the things they don't talk about. ### Sensory Fixes **Gross specificity in cooking:** - The mutants have fungal growths that burst when you cut them - The meat is fibrous in strange ways—some parts like pork, some like nothing recognizable - The spores make your throat itch even when the meat is cooked - The blood isn't quite red—it's darker, with flecks in it - Cleaning up after is its own horror—the rags, the water, the remains **Hunger that's ugly:** - Marta isn't just "light-headed." She's irritable. She can't focus. She thinks about food constantly in ways that embarrass her. - The first time she eats mutant meat, she overeats and makes herself sick. The indulgence is desperate and gross. - She catches herself eyeing the mutants differently—not as threats, as supplies. ### Tone Fixes **Let humor be weird, not wry:** - Dylan makes jokes about the mutants that are too dark. No one laughs. - Cass has no sense of humor. She takes everything literally. This creates tension. - Marta laughs at the wrong moments. It's uncomfortable. **Let dialogue be messy:** - People interrupt each other - They reference things the reader doesn't understand - They have conversations that go nowhere - They fail to communicate important things **Let some things go unexplained:** - Don't explain what the spores are - Don't explain how the outbreak started - Don't explain where the mutants come from - The not-knowing is part of the world
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Protagonist:** Marta, 34, was a prep cook who worked her way up to the line at a farm-to-table restaurant. She never went to culinary school and always felt like a fraud—but she has hands that know what to do, a palate she trusts, and a perfectionist streak that survival hasn't cured. She lost her partner in the first wave of the spores; she doesn't talk about it. She has a bad back from a decade on her feet. She alphabetizes her spices even though half the jars are empty. Cooking used to be her joy; now it's her function, and she's not sure how she feels about that. **Supporting Cast:** - **Dylan**, 19, who was an English lit dropout working at a coffee shop when everything ended. He's bad at hunting—genuinely, embarrassingly bad—and he knows it. He collects books he finds while scavenging but doesn't read them. He has guilt about someone he couldn't save, someone he won't name. He protects Lily because it's the only thing he's sure is right. - **Lily**, 8, who has coped by becoming strange. She collects small objects from the mutants—a button, a zipper, a child's hair clip—and keeps them in a box under her bed. She doesn't ask what they're eating. She already knows. She asks other questions instead. - **Cass**, 42, who was a preschool teacher with no survival skills whatsoever. She's learned by failing repeatedly, and it's made her sharp and defensive. She questions everything but contributes little. She has nightmares about her students, the ones she couldn't protect. She doesn't want to be here but has nowhere else. **Power Dynamics:** Marta controls the food. This is real power, and it creates tension. Dylan and Lily need her; they're not always grateful. Cass chooses to stay but resents the dependency. None of them would choose each other in any other world. They're bound by circumstance, appetite, and the specific thing they're all avoiding talking about. **Plot Arc:** Marta, starving and alone, discovers the mutants are edible. She begins cooking them—first for survival, then because she's good at it, then because it gives her purpose. Survivors arrive; she feeds them. A fragile household forms around her table. The horror isn't hidden from the reader, but the characters avoid looking directly at it. Questions are asked that have no good answers. A birthday is celebrated. A winter is survived. Spring comes. The story ends without resolution—just continuation, a meal, and the small tremor of a hand holding a spoon. **Voice:** First person from Marta's perspective. Wry, specific, tired. She notices food things—the way a mushroom takes butter, how salt wakes up flavor—applied to impossible ingredients without sentimentality. She doesn't explain her feelings; she describes her hands, her back, the taste in her mouth. Her narration should make the reader hungry and disturbed in the same bite. **Tone:** The cozy is real: warm kitchens, full bellies, the comfort of routine. The horror is also real: what they're eating, what it used to be, the questions they won't ask. Neither cancels the other. The story holds both without resolving. The reader should feel comforted and complicit at once. **What I Will Avoid:** - Characters who process their emotions cleanly or come to neat acceptances - Found family that feels like a gift rather than a negotiation - Dramatic reveals of humanity in the mutants; instead, small details accumulate - Moral clarity from any character, especially Cass - Resolution of the central ethical tension - Aestheticized cooking scenes; the work is practical, tiring, sometimes gross - Dialogue where everyone says what they mean --- # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: "Hunger" Marta has been three days without food. The physical reality is ugly: she's lightheaded, irritable, can't stop thinking about the last meal she ate (canned peaches, the syrup gone rancid). A shambler—a spore mutant, fungal growths pulsing along its spine—appears on the property. She kills it out of habit, not thinking. When she tries to drag the body away to burn it, she's too weak. She sets a fire right there. The smell hits her: meat cooking, fat rendering, something awakening in her that she thought was long dead. She cuts a piece from the charred remains. Cooks it more thoroughly than necessary. Eats it with her hands. It's good. Rich, complex, satisfying in a way that fills something beyond her stomach. The chapter ends with her full, sitting beside the cooling fire, understanding what she's done and not knowing how to feel about it. Her last thought: *needs rosemary*. **Key beats:** Physical desperation rendered ugly, the kill, the smell as trigger, the first meal, the dark comedy of culinary instincts surfacing. --- ## Chapter 2: "Butchery" Marta processes the remaining carcass. The work is detailed, practical, gross: the fungal growths burst when cut wrong, the meat is fibrous in strange ways, the blood is dark with flecks in it. She finds a wedding ring embedded in the flesh of a hand and puts it in her pocket without examining it. She doesn't let herself think. She salts the meat, smokes some over a slow fire, packs the root cellar. Her hands know what to do. That night, she sets the table with a cloth napkin and the good dishes—the ones that survived—and eats a proper meal alone. It feels like a ritual. It feels like grief. It feels like survival. She falls asleep in the kitchen chair, the ring heavy in her pocket. **Key beats:** The physical reality of butchery, the ring as something she can't engage with, cooking as avoidance, the domestic ritual of a meal alone. --- ## Chapter 3: "The Boy" Dylan appears trying to steal from the root cellar. Marta catches him. He's young, thin, terrified, and armed with a kitchen knife he clearly doesn't know how to use. She disarms him easily and, instead of driving him off, feeds him. He eats without asking what it is—too hungry to care. When he's full, he mentions he has a sister. Won't say where. The negotiation is awkward: he'll work, she'll feed him. Neither trusts the other. Neither has a better option. He sleeps in the barn that night; she sleeps in the house with a chair pushed under the door handle. She doesn't tell him what he's eaten. He doesn't ask. **Key beats:** Dylan's arrival as disruption, the first meal shared, the incomplete negotiation of terms, mutual wariness, the thing neither mentions. --- ## Chapter 4: "The Sister" Lily appears at the kitchen door three days later—she'd been hiding in the barn the whole time. She's small, watchful, asks no questions about the food. Cass arrives that afternoon, drawn by smoke from the chimney, asking questions Cass has no intention of answering. Marta shows Cass the meat in the root cellar, explains what it is. Cass is horrified, then hungry. That night, the first group meal: Dylan eats without thinking, Lily eats with appetite, Cass eats with visible reluctance, Marta eats the way she always does now—without letting herself consider too closely. Lily asks if the creatures used to be people. No one answers. The silence holds. **Key beats:** Lily's strange presence, Cass's horrified compromise, the first communal meal, the question no one answers, the family forming around what they won't name. --- ## Chapter 5: "Scarcity" Late autumn. The stores are lower than Marta calculated. They need to hunt. Dylan comes along and proves he's terrible at it—loud, clumsy, scared. Marta does the killing. The mutant she takes down is wearing a faded t-shirt with a university logo. She doesn't point this out. Dylan doesn't seem to notice, or chooses not to. They field-dress it together, his hands shaking, her hands steady. Back home, Cass has been "organizing" (criticizing, really) the kitchen. The meal that night is quiet. Dylan drinks too much of the bad wine someone found. Lily watches Marta butcher the meat with genuine interest. Cass stares at her plate. **Key beats:** Dylan's inadequacy exposed, the university shirt as human trace unremarked, the friction with Cass, Lily's strange interest, the weight of what they're doing settling over the table. --- ## Chapter 6: "Birthday" Lily's ninth birthday. Dylan remembered; Marta didn't. There are no gifts, but Marta makes something special—braised meat with the last of her good spices, root vegetables from the garden, a cake of sorts from scavenged flour and dried fruit. The meal is warm. They're all trying. Cass drinks too much again and says, "We're going to hell, you know. All of us." Dylan tells her to shut up. Lily keeps eating. Marta watches her hands shake as she cuts Lily a second piece of cake. No one apologizes. Everyone sleeps badly. **Key beats:** The effort at celebration, the warmth that's real, Cass breaking the silence with something no one wants to hear, the moment that fractures and heals around the edges, Lily at the center of it—eating, always eating. --- ## Chapter 7: "Deep Winter" January. The mutants have gone dormant or migrated; food is scarce again. Cass announces she's thinking of leaving. She doesn't say why, doesn't say where. Dylan gets angry—she'd be dead out there. Lily asks if Cass is going to become one of the food-creatures. Cass doesn't answer. No one leaves. The tension is thick for days. A mutant finally appears in the yard during a thaw—weak, slow, almost easy. Marta kills it without the others. In its pocket: a folded photograph. She puts it in her pocket with the ring. She doesn't look at it. She doesn't throw it away. **Key beats:** The threat of dissolution, the cold that drives everyone inward, Lily's impossible question, the photograph Marta carries but won't see, the choice to continue without examining why. --- ## Chapter 8: "Thaw" Spring. The crisis has passed, though nothing is resolved. Marta plants a garden. Cass is still here—miserable, critical, staying. Dylan practices hunting and gets slightly less terrible. Lily shows Marta the collection under her bed: buttons, a zipper, a child's plastic barrette, all salvaged from mutants. She asks if Marta thinks the creatures know they used to be people. Marta says she doesn't know. It's the truest answer she has. The chapter ends with dinner: the four of them at the table, bread Marta made, soup thick with vegetables and meat. It's warm. It's wrong. They're all still here. Marta's hand shakes slightly as she lifts her spoon. The soup is delicious. She keeps eating. **Key beats:** The return of life, Cass choosing to stay without explaining why, Lily's collection as the evidence she's been gathering, the question Marta answers honestly, the final meal as continuation—warmth and wrongness held together, no resolution, just appetite and the people bound by it.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Marta **Background & Personality:** Marta grew up watching her grandmother cook—real cooking, the kind where you taste with your fingers and know by smell when something's done. Her grandmother had no recipes, just hands that knew. Marta spent a decade trying to learn that same instinct. She worked her way up from dishwasher to prep cook to the line at a farm-to-table restaurant that was prettier than it was successful. She never went to culinary school, which meant she always felt like a fraud, even when the head chef told her she had the best palate in the kitchen. She lived with Anna, who worked in insurance and didn't understand why Marta cared so much about whether the onions were properly caramelized. Anna died in the first month of the spores—got infected, turned, and Marta put her down with a meat cleaver in their kitchen. She hasn't spoken her name aloud since. She hasn't entered the bedroom they shared. Marta is exacting and controlling in the kitchen because the kitchen is the only place she knows what she's doing. Outside of it, she's uncertain, irritable, prone to overcorrecting. She has a perfectionist streak that survival hasn't softened—if anything, the apocalypse has made her meaner about doing things "right." She's not cruel, just exacting in ways that feel cruel when you're on the receiving end. **Idiosyncrasies & Flaws:** - Alphabetizes her spices even though half the jars are empty. The organizational system matters more than the contents. - Has a specific chipped mug that says "World's Okayest Cook" in faded letters—a joke gift from a coworker years ago. No one else is allowed to use it. She'll know if they do. - Hums the same four bars of a song while she cooks, over and over. She doesn't remember the rest of the song or where she learned it. It's muscle memory. - Saves seeds from every vegetable she encounters, dries them carefully, stores them in labeled envelopes. She's terrible at gardening. They never sprout. - Cannot tolerate other people in her kitchen. Their presence makes the space feel wrong, contaminated. She allows it because she has to, but her shoulders tense. - Hoards salt. Has a jar buried in the backyard "just in case." Doesn't know what case she's saving it for. - Judges people who can't cook as morally deficient. **Physicality:** Marta is 34, though she looks older from exhaustion and younger from the baby fat that never left her face. She's built like someone who's spent a decade on her feet—stocky, thick-shouldered, lower back that aches constantly. Her hands are rough and scarred: a burn along her left forearm from a grill accident, nicks on her fingers from slipped knives. Brown hair she cuts herself with kitchen shears, uneven and practical. Moves economically, no wasted motion—the instinct of someone who learned that efficiency means survival. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Marta's primary drive is purpose. Survival without meaning is just prolonged dying, and she knows it. Cooking gives her a reason to get up. She needs to be needed. But she also resents being needed—resents that these strangers have claimed her, her space, her work. She's not a natural caretaker. She doesn't soften her voice for comfort. She cares for people by feeding them, which is the only language of love she has, and sometimes it's not enough. She's protective of Lily but doesn't know how to talk to children. She respects Dylan's effort and is frustrated by his incompetence. She has the most complicated relationship with Cass—two adult women with no illusions about each other, stuck together. Cass sees through Marta's functional purpose. Marta hates that. She hasn't examined whether what they're eating is right. She doesn't let herself. The ring in her pocket is Anna's. The photograph isn't. **Voice Samples:** *Internal monologue:* > "The meat wanted acid. Everything wants acid if you listen long enough. Tomatoes, slow-cooked until they gave up. A splash of the bad wine—the vinegar that pretended it was still drinkable. The mushrooms took the brightness better than expected, or maybe I'd just forgotten what expected meant." *Dialogue:* > "You're holding it wrong. Not wrong like there's a right way—wrong like you're hoping it'll do the work for you. It won't. Nothing does." --- # Dylan **Background & Personality:** Dylan was two years into an English literature degree when he dropped out, told everyone it was to "figure things out," and ended up working at a coffee shop where he memorized people's orders because he was good at small, useless details. He reads poetry in his head when he's scared—Auden, mostly, sometimes Yeats. He doesn't tell anyone this because it would require explaining, and explaining would require being known. When the spores came, he was living with Marcus, his best friend since high school. Marcus got infected on a supply run. Dylan locked him out of the apartment and listened to him bang on the door for three hours before the sounds changed. He tells people he lost everyone. He doesn't say he chose who to lose. Dylan wants to be useful. He's desperate for it. He tries too hard, overthinks everything, gets in his own way. He uses humor to defuse tension and sometimes makes things worse. He's not brave; he's just good at performing bravery until the moment he isn't. **Idiosyncrasies & Flaws:** - Collects books while scavenging but never reads them. His room has a small stack—poetry, a field guide to birds, a self-help book from the 90s. He likes having them. He doesn't know why. - Has a pen in his pocket at all times. No paper. Just the pen. He fidgets with the cap. - Apologizes too much, even when he hasn't done anything wrong. "Sorry" is his filler word. - Lies about small things to seem more capable. Says he's found trails when he's gotten lost. Says he's fine when he's not. - Can't handle silence. Fills it with chatter, observations, questions no one asked. - Moves too quietly on purpose, trying to be stealthy, which just makes people nervous when he appears behind them. **Physicality:** Nineteen, tall in a way he hasn't grown into yet—shoulders hunched forward like he's trying to take up less space.
