Thin from rationing, with knobby wrists and a face that still looks young despite everything. Dark hair he cuts himself, badly; there's always a section too short or too long. Constantly moves: shifting weight, tapping fingers, picking at his cuticles. Eye contact is hard for him. He looks at people's mouths when they talk, then feels weird about it. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Dylan's primary drive is Lily—keeping her alive is the only penance that makes sense, even if he doesn't frame it that way. He stayed in the apartment listening to Marcus because Lily was in the next room. He always chooses Lily. But he also resents her, just a little, for being another person he can't fail. He's grateful to Marta, which makes him feel pathetic. He wants her approval and knows he'll never fully get it. He's intimidated by Cass, who sees through him, and he tries to stay out of her way. He doesn't think too hard about what they're eating. He can't. If he thinks about it, he'll think about Marcus—whether Marcus is out there now, whether someone else is— He doesn't think about it. **Voice Samples:** *Internal monologue:* > "There's a line from Auden about the dead, how they are still living in our sense, but that wasn't right either. The dead weren't living anywhere. They were just gone, or worse than gone, walking around with mushrooms growing out of their backs. The poem didn't cover that." *Dialogue:* > "I found these. They were in a house about a mile east—cans, I mean, not... yeah. Cans. I can go back if there's more you need. I didn't check the upstairs because the stairs looked, um, not there anymore. But I can figure something out. I'll figure it out." --- # Lily **Background & Personality:** Lily was in second grade when the world ended. She remembers her teacher's name (Ms. Castellanos), the poster on the wall (a cat hanging from a branch, "Hang in There!"), and the smell of the hand sanitizer everyone used before snack time. She doesn't talk about her parents. Dylan says they didn't make it, which is the kind of thing adults say when they don't want to explain. Lily understands more than people think. She's learned that adults lie to protect themselves, not her. She's become strange in ways that worry Dylan and fascinate Marta. She doesn't flinch at the mutants. She watches them with curiosity, the same way she watches Marta cook—studying, learning. She collects things. She asks questions. She hasn't cried since the first month, which might mean she's processing or might mean she's not. **Idiosyncrasies & Flaws:** - Keeps a collection in a shoebox under her bed: a button, a zipper, a child's plastic barrette, a broken watch, a folded piece of paper she won't let anyone read. All salvaged from mutants. She doesn't explain why. - Eats very quickly, like the food might be taken away. Always wants seconds. - Hums while she eats. The same tune as Marta, though neither has noticed. - Doesn't like being touched without warning. Will flinch if someone moves too fast. - Asks one impossible question per day, at random moments. "Do they dream?" "What happens if you eat the mushrooms?" "Was this one a mom?" - Insists on being useful. Wants to help cook. Dylan won't let her near the knives. **Physicality:** Eight years old, small for her age, with tangled brown hair she refuses to let anyone brush properly. Her fingernails are bitten to the quick. She moves carefully, placing each foot deliberately, like she's testing the ground. Wide eyes that watch everything. She sits very still when she's thinking, which is often. People forget she's in the room because she makes herself quiet and small. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Lily's primary drive is understanding. She wants to know what the mutants are, what they were, what they might still be. She's not morbid—just practical. These are the facts of her world, and she wants to have all the information. She loves Dylan in a fierce, complicated way. He's safe, he's familiar, but he also lies to protect her. She knows this. It makes her trust him less and need him more. She watches Marta with interest—the only adult who doesn't try to explain things away. Marta tells the truth when asked directly. Lily values this. Cass is confusing to her. Cass says things like "we're going to hell" and then doesn't explain what that means. Lily has started tracking Cass's drinking, counting how many glasses, noting what it makes her say. **Voice Samples:** *Internal monologue:* > "The mushroom on the last one was purple at the edges. Marta cut it off and threw it in the fire, but I saw where it had been growing. The skin underneath was softer. Sensitive. Like it would have hurt if the mushroom was still there." *Dialogue:* > "What's the difference between a person who dies and a person who turns? Is there a difference? I'm not trying to be difficult. I just want to know the rules." --- # Cass **Background & Personality:** Cass taught preschool for fifteen years. She believed in the work. She had a classroom with a reading corner and a sensory table and a behavior chart with the children's names on colorful clothespins. She was good at it—patient, warm, the kind of teacher parents requested. She was divorced at 39, no kids of her own, and told herself the classroom was enough. When the spores came, she was at school with twelve children. Three parents came. She sent the other nine home to parents who never arrived, or who arrived already changing. She doesn't know what happened to most of them. She assumes. She doesn't let herself do more than assume. Cass is brittle now. Survival has stripped away the softness she used to wear. She questions everything—Marta's choices, the food supply, the ethics of staying, the point of continuing. She drinks when there's alcohol. She says true things at wrong moments. She's not mean, just unfiltered in ways that feel mean. She knows she's hard to be around. She hasn't decided if she cares. **Idiosyncrasies & Flaws:** - Hoards small comforts—extra blanket, a tin of tea she's saving for something she can't name. She hides them in her room like contraband. - Says the quiet thing out loud. "We're going to hell." "This one had a wedding ring." Observations that land like stones. - Can't cook. Genuinely, spectacularly bad at it. Burns water. Over-salts everything. Marta banned her from the kitchen after the soup incident. - Fidgets with a lighter she found—clicks it open and closed, open and closed. The flame is long dead. - Has nightmares and won't talk about them. Wakes up gasping, then refuses to acknowledge it happened. - Apologizes for nothing, even when she should. "Sorry" isn't in her vocabulary anymore. **Physicality:** Forty-two, though the lines around her eyes make her look older. Average height, going soft around the middle from months of reduced activity and preserved food. Gray threading through brown hair she pulls back in a severe ponytail. Dressed in layers—a tank top, a flannel, a jacket she refuses to remove even inside, like she's always ready to leave. She holds herself tight, arms often crossed, shoulders raised. Drinks with both hands on the cup. **Motives, Allegiances, Relationships:** Cass stays because leaving means dying, and dying means all of this—the survival, the compromises, the things she's eaten—meant nothing. She needs there to be meaning. She doesn't believe there is. She's hardest on Marta because Marta has made peace with what they're doing. Cass can't. She participates anyway, and that self-betrayal makes her cruel. She sees through Dylan's attempts at bravery. She sees Lily watching her and doesn't know what to do with being seen. She's thought about leaving. She stays. **Voice Samples:** *Internal monologue:* > "There should be a word for what we're doing. Cannibalism isn't right—they're not people anymore, or that's what I tell myself when the meat is in my mouth. There should be a word. I don't want to invent it. I don't want that to be my contribution to whatever's left of language." *Dialogue:* > "You want to know the truth? The truth is I've killed three of them—three of those things—and I don't remember any of their faces. I remember what the first one was wearing. A yellow dress. A child's dress. And I don't remember her face."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: Hunger Three days without food. The first day you pretend it's fine. The second day you move slower, conserve. The third day you start to understand why people used to do terrible things. I was lying on the kitchen floor because it was cooler there, the tile pressed against my cheek, when I heard it outside. The shuffling. The wet, rattling breath. A spore mutant. A shambler, the surviving called them, though most of the people who'd called them that were dead now. I should have stayed down. That was the smart play. Shamblers wandered. They passed through. If you were quiet, if you were lucky, they moved on to somewhere else. But I was so hungry. And somewhere in the back of my skull, beneath the dizziness and the hollow ache, a small voice said: *what if it doesn't leave? What if it stays right outside, and I have to listen to it for hours, and I can't get to the root cellar?* The root cellar. Two jars of pickled beets. A can of tomatoes with a dent in the side so deep I'd been saving it, telling myself I'd eat it last. That was what was left. I pushed myself up. My arms shook. The room tilted, then steadied. Through the window, I could see it in the yard. The shambler was bent over something—my garden, I realized. The dead stalks of my failed tomatoes. It was pawing at the dirt with hands that had stopped looking like hands months ago. The fungal growths along its spine pulsed in the gray afternoon light. Pale. Spongy. Like the underside of a mushroom. I had a machete by the door. Everyone had weapons by the doors now. I went outside. The cold hit me first—late autumn, the kind where the chill seeps through whatever you're wearing and settles into your joints. I was in a coat I'd taken off a dead man three towns over. It didn't fit right. The arms were too long. The shambler didn't notice me until I was close. Then it turned, and I saw its face. It had been a woman once. The features were still there, underneath. But the spores had bloomed inside her, pushed out through the soft tissue. White threads threaded through her cheeks. Her eyes were clouded, pupil and iris swallowed by something fungal and spreading. She made a sound—not a word, not a scream. Just a wet push of air through a throat that didn't work right anymore. I swung the machete. The blade caught her at the base of the skull. The body dropped. The mushrooms along her spine burst when she hit the ground, releasing a cloud of spores I tried not to breathe. I stepped back, coughing, wiping my face. Then I just stood there. I should have dragged the body away. Should have burned it. That was protocol. You didn't leave shamblers lying around; the spores could spread, contaminate the soil, get into the water. I knew this. Everyone knew this. But I was so tired. And so hungry. And the body was heavy, and my arms felt like they were made of something that wasn't quite muscle anymore. I considered just leaving it. Going inside. Lying back down on the kitchen floor and waiting to feel better. But I'd killed it. That meant something. I didn't know what, but it felt wrong to just walk away. I dragged the body to the fire pit I'd made in the backyard—a ring of stones, a pile of wood I'd been saving for winter. I couldn't lift the shambler into it. I tried twice, and my back screamed, and I had to sit down in the dead grass and breathe. Finally, I just built the fire around the body. Jammed kindling underneath its arms, its legs. It felt wrong. It felt like something I shouldn't be doing, shouldn't be seeing. The fire caught. The wood crackled. And then— The smell. I don't know what I expected. Rot, maybe. Something sour and spoiled. That was what the shamblers smelled like alive: damp earth and something chemical underneath. But cooking, the meat smelled like *meat*. My mouth watered. I couldn't stop it. My body responded before my mind could catch up, before I could remember what I was smelling, *who* I might be smelling. Pork, maybe. Or something close to it. Rich and fatty, with an undertone of mushroom—earthy, deep. I stared at the fire. At the body charring inside it. *This is insane*, I thought. I was still thinking it when I reached into the flames. I burned my hand. I didn't care. I pulled a piece of the creature free—a chunk of thigh, the meat beneath the skin dark and steaming. It was hot enough to hurt when I gripped it, but I couldn't make myself let go. I took it inside. Put it on the cutting board. Stared at it. The exterior was charred, blackened from the fire. The inside was pink. Just like pork. Just like any other cut of meat you'd pull from an oven. *Don't think about it*, I told myself. *Don't think about the face. Don't think about what it used to be.* I cut a piece. Small. Bite-sized. I put it in my mouth. The taste hit me before the reasoning could. It was *good*. Rich and savory, with a depth I hadn't tasted in months. The fungal notes weren't unpleasant—they added something, a complexity that made me think of truffle oil, of the fancy dishes I used to prep at the restaurant. I chewed. Swallowed. Ate another piece. I ate until the chunk was gone. Then I went back outside, to the dying fire, and pulled more meat from the body. I didn't let myself think about what I was doing. I didn't let myself remember the woman's face, the cloudy eyes, the white threads threading through her cheeks. I just ate. When I was full—truly full, for the first time in weeks—I sat back in the kitchen chair and stared at the bones on my plate. Outside, the sun was setting. The fire had burned down to embers. The remains of the shambler were still out there, half-charred, picked clean in places. I'd eaten a person. Maybe. Probably. I didn't know what the spores did, exactly—whether the person was still in there, whether they'd been gone for months. I didn't know if it mattered. What I knew was this: my hands had stopped shaking. My head had cleared. The gnawing pit in my stomach had closed. I looked at the bones again. *Needs rosemary*, I thought. *And acid. Something to cut the fat.* Then I put my head in my hands and laughed until it sounded like crying.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements. **Voice and Perspective:** The first-person voice is well-calibrated. Marta's narration is wry, specific, and tired without being performatively so. The line "The first day you pretend it's fine. The second day you move slower, conserve. The third day you start to understand why people used to do terrible things" is economical and effective—it establishes her pragmatic worldview without over-explaining. The shift to second person in the opening is a minor stylistic choice that works reasonably well, though it's a slightly familiar technique. **The Central Horror:** The chapter handles the central conceit with genuine skill. The smell as trigger is rendered effectively—the body's response preceding the mind's is psychologically accurate and avoids melodrama. The moment of eating is appropriately matter-of-fact without being gratuitously clinical. The chapter earns its darkness. **The Ending:** "Needs rosemary. And acid. Something to cut the fat." followed by laughing until it sounds like crying is the chapter's best moment. It's exactly what the plan called for—dark comedy of culinary instincts surfacing—and it lands. This is the kind of tonal balance the prompt demands, and it's achieved without forcing it. **Weaknesses:** The chapter is somewhat predictable in its structure. The beats follow the plan closely but don't surprise beyond it. The description of the shambler's face ("white threads threaded through her cheeks") is competent but not particularly distinctive—the visual language of fungal horror is well-trodden territory and the chapter doesn't find a fresh angle on it. The phrase "white threads threaded" is a minor inelegance. The line "I'd eaten a person. Maybe. Probably. I didn't know what the spores did, exactly" is the chapter's most on-the-nose moment—it tells us what Marta is thinking rather than letting the action carry the weight. The plan specifically called for avoiding characters who process emotions cleanly, and this passage comes close to violating that. **Dialogue:** There is no dialogue in this chapter, which is appropriate given Marta is alone. The internal monologue functions as the voice. **Consistency with Character Profile:** Marta's physicality (bad back, rough hands, economic movement) is present. Her culinary instincts surfacing at the worst possible moment is perfectly in character. The chapter doesn't yet show her alphabetized spices or her specific idiosyncrasies, but this is the first chapter and she's starving—those details would feel forced here. **Following the Plan:** The chapter hits all the planned beats: physical desperation rendered ugly, the kill, the smell as trigger, the first meal, the dark comedy of culinary instincts. The "needs rosemary" ending is executed as planned. The chapter is faithful to the plan without being mechanical about it. **Prose Quality:** Generally clean and controlled. The writing avoids purple prose almost entirely. There are occasional moments of slight over-explanation ("I didn't let myself think about what I was doing. I didn't let myself remember the woman's face") that could be trimmed. The coat taken from a dead man is a nice detail. The failed tomatoes in the garden is good—it establishes her gardening incompetence from the character profile without announcing it. **Tonal Balance:** The cozy-horror balance is present but the "cozy" element is minimal in this chapter, which is appropriate—this is the origin chapter, the desperate moment before any warmth is established. The horror is real without being gratuitous. Overall, this is a competent-to-good opening chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains a consistent and appropriate voice, and earns its central dark moment. It doesn't transcend its plan, but it doesn't fail it either. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: Butchery The next morning, I went back outside to finish what I'd started. The shambler's remains were still in the fire pit, scattered now—something had been at them in the night. A raccoon, maybe, or a stray dog. The ribs were exposed, picked clean in places, and the skull had rolled away into the dead grass. I stood over the mess with the machete in my hand and tried to remember how to think like a cook. The thigh meat was what I'd eaten. That much I knew. There'd be more in the other leg, the lower back. The shoulders if the muscle hadn't spoiled. The organs I'd leave—too much risk of contamination, the spores concentrated in the soft tissue. The brain, the heart, the lungs. Those I'd burn separately, buried deep. But the muscle. The meat. That was usable. *Usable*. I was already thinking in that word. Already making it practical. I dragged what was left of the body to the shed I'd been using for storage. It smelled like motor oil and old hay, and there was a workbench along one wall that would work as a surface. I'd butchered animals before, back at the restaurant—whole pigs, lambs in the spring. The principles were the same. I told myself that, anyway. The principles were the same. The first cut was along the spine. I worked the machete like a cleaver, heavy and deliberate, separating the halves. The fungal growths burst when I pressed too hard, releasing a puff of something pale and powdery. I held my breath until it settled, then wiped the blade on my coat. The meat underneath was strange. The muscle fibers ran in directions that didn't quite make sense—crosshatched in places, bundled tight in others. The fat was yellowed, more dense than pork fat, closer to what I remembered of duck. The blood was dark, nearly black, with flecks of something suspended in it that caught the light wrong. I worked methodically. That was the only way to do it. If I thought too much about what I was cutting—about the shape of the hand I trimmed around, the architecture of the shoulder blade—I'd have to stop. And I couldn't stop. I'd already started, and I was still hungry, and there was a winter coming that I wasn't going to survive on pickled beets and dented tomatoes. I found the ring when I was stripping the meat from the left hand. It caught on my knife—a small resistance, metal against metal. I paused. Pulled the blade back. The ring was wedged between two fingers, the skin around it swollen and discolored. A wedding band. Simple. Gold, or something like it, dulled by months of exposure. I stared at it for a long moment. Then I pried it loose with the tip of my knife and put it in my pocket without looking too closely. I didn't need to see the inscription if there was one. I didn't need to know if it was engraved with a date, a name, something that would make this particular body into a particular person. It was in my pocket now. That was enough. I kept working. By midday, I had a pile of usable meat—maybe eight pounds total, trimmed and rinsed in water I'd heated on the woodstove. The shed smelled like blood and something else, something I didn't have a word for. Mushroom and meat and the chemical undertone of the spores. I built a small fire in the pit behind the house—a clean one this time, just wood, no bodies. I needed to smoke what I couldn't eat fresh. The process was simple. Salt first, rubbed into every surface. I'd been hoarding salt for months, traded for it on supply runs, stolen it from houses that didn't need it anymore. I had a jar buried in the backyard and another hidden in the root cellar, and now I used a third of what I had left on meat that used to be a woman. The salt made it real. Salting meat was preservation. It meant planning for the future, believing there would be a future to plan for. I hung the cuts from string I'd scavenged, suspended them over a rack I'd built from old fencing. The fire was small, just enough to generate smoke. I'd used hickory when I could find it, oak when I couldn't. Today it was oak, split thin, burning slow. While the meat smoked, I went inside and washed my hands three times. The water ran gray, then pink, then clear. The smell lingered under my fingernails. I scrubbed with dish soap I'd been rationing, then with a paste of salt and baking soda. My hands were raw when I was done, the skin pink and tender, but I could still smell it. Maybe it wasn't on my hands. Maybe it was just in my head. That night, I set the table. This was stupid, probably. I was alone. There was no one to set the table for. But I did it anyway—the cloth napkins I'd found in a department store six months ago, still in their packaging. The good dishes, salvaged from my own kitchen before I'd left the city. A candle I'd been saving, beeswax, expensive. I plated the fresh meat carefully. A cut from the loin, seared in the last of my butter, finished with a splash of the bad vinegar I'd been using for everything. I'd found wild rosemary growing by the creek two weeks ago—I'd dried it, stored it, and now I crumbled it over the plate with fingers that knew exactly how much to use. The meat was better than the night before. The resting period had let the flavors settle, the fibers relax. It was tender. Rich. The mushroom notes were balanced now, earthy instead of strange. I ate at the table with the cloth napkin in my lap and the candle burning between me and nothing. It felt like a ritual. It felt like grief. It felt like survival. I didn't think about Anna—not directly. I didn't let myself picture her face, her hands, the way she'd looked at me when I told her I was just going to check the perimeter, just going to be a minute, I'd be right back. I didn't think about that. I just ate. When I was done, I washed the plate, dried it, put it back in the cabinet. I blew out the candle and saved the stub for another night. I walked through the dark house to my room and sat on the edge of the bed without turning on the lantern. The ring was still in my pocket. I didn't take it out. I didn't look at it. I just sat there in the dark, feeling the weight of it through the fabric, and tried not to think about anything at all. Sleep came eventually—a thin, restless thing. I dreamed of kitchens, of prep work, of the way a knife feels when it's perfectly sharp. I dreamed of my grandmother's hands, the way she'd test a sauce with her pinky finger, the way she never wrote anything down. I woke with my face pressed into the pillow and the ring still in my pocket, and the first thought in my head was: *the smokehouse needs tending.* So I got up. And went outside. And tended the meat.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this piece is a strong execution of a difficult premise. Let me assess it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The voice is remarkably consistent and controlled. Marta's narration is exactly what the plan promised: wry, specific, tired, and grounded in the physical reality of cooking. Lines like "Salting meat was preservation. It meant planning for the future, believing there would be a future to plan for" work because they earn their weight through the context of the butchery scene rather than arriving as imposed profundity. The observation is character-specific and thematically resonant without being overwrought. The butchery scene itself is handled with admirable restraint. The detail about the fungal growths bursting and releasing "something pale and powdery" is specific and unsettling without being gratuitous. The description of the meat's strange muscle fiber arrangement and the dark blood with suspended flecks is genuinely creative—it makes the reader feel the wrongness without belaboring it. The ring is the chapter's central object, and it's handled with exactly the right touch. Marta finds it, pries it loose, puts it in her pocket "without looking too closely." She explicitly refuses to read any inscription. This is showing rather than telling—we understand her psychological state through what she refuses to do rather than through any internal declaration. The final image of the ring's weight through fabric is earned. The dinner scene is the chapter's emotional core, and it works. The cloth napkins, the good dishes, the beeswax candle—these domestic details accumulate into something genuinely moving. "It felt like a ritual. It felt like grief. It felt like survival." This triple construction could easily tip into purple prose, but it doesn't quite, because each term is doing distinct work and the rhythm is controlled. It's the closest the chapter comes to over-writing, but it lands. The Anna avoidance is handled well. "I didn't think about Anna—not directly" followed by the specific memory she refuses to complete ("I'd be right back") is psychologically precise. The reader gets the shape of the wound without the wound being displayed. The ending—waking up, the ring still in the pocket, the first thought being the smokehouse—is excellent. It captures Marta's character perfectly: survival as function, purpose as avoidance, the body's demands overriding the mind's grief. WEAKNESSES: The chapter has almost no dialogue, which is appropriate given Marta is alone, but it means we're entirely in her head. The plan called for "cooking as avoidance" and "the domestic ritual of a meal alone," both of which are executed, but the chapter is somewhat static in terms of event. This is a chapter about processing and preparation, and it succeeds at that, but it doesn't quite have the narrative momentum that would make it truly compelling as a plot chapter. The dream sequence near the end is the weakest passage. "I dreamed of kitchens, of prep work, of the way a knife feels when it's perfectly sharp. I dreamed of my grandmother's hands" is functional but not particularly distinctive. It's the one moment where the writing becomes slightly generic—the grandmother's hands, the sauce tested with a pinky finger. These are real details but they feel like they're reaching for significance rather than finding it organically. The line "Maybe it wasn't on my hands. Maybe it was just in my head" is the chapter's most obvious moment—a bit too neat as a psychological observation, slightly on-the-nose about the nature of guilt and contamination. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the butchery detail, the ring found and pocketed without examination, the smoking of meat, the domestic ritual of a meal alone, the ring's weight at the end. All plan beats are hit. The prose is generally clean and controlled. There's no significant purple prose, and the metaphors that appear are grounded in Marta's culinary perspective, which keeps them from feeling forced. The piece is not amateurish. It's the work of a writer who understands restraint. The darkness is well-earned throughout. The horror accumulates through specificity rather than declaration, and the cozy elements (the cloth napkins, the candle, the rosemary) genuinely coexist with the horror rather than canceling it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: The Boy I found him in the root cellar at two in the morning. I wasn't sleeping well. Never slept well anymore—the dreams were always wrong, or the silence was too loud, or my back ached in that dull, constant way that meant rain was coming. I'd gotten up to check the smokehouse, to make sure the fire hadn't died, when I heard the noise from behind the house. Footsteps. Careless and loud. I grabbed the machete from beside the door and moved toward the sound. The root cellar door was open—a rectangle of darkness in the moonlit yard. Someone was down there. I could hear them moving, knocking against the shelves, cursing in a whisper. I waited. A figure emerged, arms full of jars. He was backlit by the moon, but I could see enough—young, thin, dressed in layers of mismatched clothing. A knit hat pulled low over his ears. A coat that was too big for his frame. And a kitchen knife tucked into his belt. He hadn't even drawn it. "Drop them," I said. He spun. A jar slipped, shattered on the ground. Pickled beets scattered across the dirt, dark and glistening. "Shit," he said. Then, quicker: "I'm sorry. I'm sorry, I was just—I didn't think anyone was—" "I said drop them." He dropped the other two jars. They didn't break. Small mercies. "Step away from the door." He stepped. His hands were up now, trembling slightly. I could see his face in the moonlight—sharp features, eyes too wide, a patchy attempt at facial hair that couldn't decide if it wanted to be a beard or not. He couldn't have been older than twenty. "I wasn't going to take much," he said. "Just enough to—I've been watching the house, and I didn't see any movement, and I thought maybe it was empty, or—" "You've been watching the house." He flinched. "Not in a creepy way. Just—I wanted to make sure it was safe before I—look, I'm sorry. I'll go. I won't come back." He was already backing up, his hands still raised, his voice pitched high with panic. The kitchen knife glinted at his hip. He hadn't reached for it once. "Stop moving," I said. He stopped. I looked at him—really looked. The way his shoulders hunched forward, like he was trying to fold in on himself. The way his eyes kept darting to the machete in my hand. The trembling that hadn't stopped. He was starving. I could see it in the hollows of his cheeks, the way his coat hung off his frame. He probably hadn't eaten in days. Maybe longer. I'd been there. Three days ago, I'd been there. "Are you alone?" I asked. A pause. Too long. "Yes." "You're lying." "I'm not—" "You're a terrible liar. You've been watching the house, which means you've been nearby for at least a day or two. There are tracks all over the woods behind the property—I've seen them. Boot prints and smaller ones. Sneakers, maybe. A child's size." His face changed. Something harder under the fear. "Stay away from her," he said. Low and quiet. So there *was* a her. "I'm not going to hurt you," I said. "Or whoever you're protecting. I'm going to put down the machete, and you're going to put down that ridiculous knife you've never used in your life, and we're going to go inside. I'm going to feed you. And then we're going to talk about why you were stealing my pickled beets." He blinked. "You're going to... feed me?" "That's what I said." "Why?" Because I remember what hunger feels like. Because I've been alone for four months and I'm starting to forget what voices sound like. Because the ring in my pocket is getting heavy and I need something to do with my hands. "Because I have food," I said. "And you clearly don't. Pick up those jars you didn't break. Put them back in the cellar. Then come inside." I turned and walked toward the house without waiting to see if he'd follow. He followed. --- The kitchen was warm—or as warm as it got, with the woodstove banked for the night. I lit the lantern and set it on the table while he stood in the doorway, watching me like I might change my mind. "Sit," I said. He sat. I pulled food from the icebox—the smoked meat I'd preserved, cut thin. Some potatoes I'd roasted two days ago, still good. A heel of bread I'd been saving. I didn't think about what I was serving him. I didn't let myself think about it. The plate went in front of him. He stared at it. "Eat," I said. He ate. He ate like a feral animal—hands shoving food into his mouth, barely chewing before swallowing. He didn't taste it. He didn't savor it. He just consumed, his body working faster than his mind could catch up. I watched him. I'd done the same thing. Three days ago, I'd done exactly the same thing. When the plate was clean, he looked up at me. His eyes were wet. I pretended not to notice. "Thank you," he said. "I'm sorry. I should have—I should have asked." "You should have." "I didn't think... I didn't think anyone would just..." His voice cracked. He pressed the heel of his hand against his eyes, hard, like he could push the feelings back inside. "The bathroom is down the hall," I said. "There's a pump for water. You smell terrible, and I'm not letting you sit at my table like that." He laughed, or tried to. It came out broken. "What's your name?" I asked. "Dylan." "Dylan. I'm Marta. You're going to sleep in the barn tonight. There are blankets in the chest by the door. Tomorrow, we'll talk about what you're going to do to earn your keep." He nodded quickly. "I can work. Whatever you need. I can—" "We'll talk tomorrow." He stood. Hovered. His mouth opened, closed. "The girl," he said finally. "My sister. She's in the woods. I didn't want to bring her until I knew—" "Bring her in the morning. Tonight, you sleep. I'll leave the barn door unlocked." He looked at me for a long moment—like he was trying to figure out what kind of trap this was, what I wanted from him, why anyone would give food to a stranger in the end of the world. I didn't have answers for him. "Thank you," he said again. Then he was gone, out the back door, into the dark. I listened to his footsteps fade toward the barn. Then I went through the house to the front door and wedged a chair under the handle. I didn't know him. I didn't trust him. But I'd fed him. In the morning, I'd feed his sister too. I lay back down in the dark, the ring pressing against my hip, and tried not to think about what I'd just done—or what I'd just started.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The chapter follows the blueprint closely: Dylan appears trying to steal from the root cellar, Marta catches him, feeds him without explaining what he's eating, he mentions a sister, and they negotiate an uneasy arrangement. The chair under the door handle at the end is a nice touch that honors the plan's note about mutual wariness. The voice is the chapter's strongest element. Marta's narration is exactly what the plan promised: wry, specific, tired. "He ate like a feral animal—hands shoving food into his mouth, barely chewing before swallowing. He didn't taste it. He didn't savor it. He just consumed, his body working faster than his mind could catch up. I watched him. I'd done the same thing. Three days ago, I'd done exactly the same thing." This is clean, controlled, and earns its emotional weight without reaching for it. The parallel between Marta's first meal and Dylan's is implicit and effective. The dialogue is largely strong. It's spare, functional, and reveals character without being on-the-nose. "You smell terrible, and I'm not letting you sit at my table like that" is perfectly in character—Marta's care expressed as criticism. Dylan's broken laugh, his hovering, his mouth opening and closing—these are well-observed behavioral details that show his character without explaining it. The chapter is notably disciplined about what it doesn't say. Dylan doesn't ask what he's eating. Marta doesn't tell him. The horror is present in the negative space. This is exactly the tonal balance the plan called for. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the internal monologue moment where Marta lists her reasons for feeding Dylan ("Because I remember what hunger feels like. Because I've been alone for four months...") is the most overtly "literary" passage and the one that most risks telling rather than showing. The reasons are good, but the list format feels slightly constructed compared to the rest of the narration. The ring appearing at the end ("the ring pressing against my hip") is a bit mechanical—it's been established as a motif, but its insertion here feels like a reminder rather than an organic recurrence. Dylan's characterization is consistent with his profile: the apologizing, the lying about being alone, the protective instinct toward Lily, the performed bravery. His voice samples from the profile ("I'll figure it out") match the energy of his dialogue here. Marta reads as entirely herself—controlling, practical, caring through function rather than warmth. The chapter doesn't quite reach the complexity of the plan's note about "the thing neither mentions"—the food goes unremarked, which is correct, but there's no moment where the reader feels the weight of that silence pressing on the scene. It's handled correctly but not with maximum effect. The chapter is competent and controlled where it might have been quietly devastating. The prose is clean throughout. No purple passages, no forced metaphors. The "pickled beets scattered across the dirt, dark and glistening" is a nice sensory detail that earns its place. The moonlit scene is rendered without cliché. Overall: a well-executed chapter that honors its plan and its characters, with a voice that's consistent and controlled. Its restraint is its virtue and occasionally its limitation—it does what it sets out to do without quite transcending it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4: The Sister She appeared in the kitchen doorway three days later, watching me. I'd been dicing the last of the smoked meat—fine, uniform cubes, the way I'd been taught. Force of habit. The knife moved on its own while my mind drifted, and I didn't notice her standing there until I reached for the salt and caught movement in my peripheral vision. She was small. Eight, maybe nine. Tangled brown hair that hadn't seen a brush in weeks, a coat that swallowed her hands, eyes that watched everything and gave nothing away. "You're not very quiet," I said. "Neither are you." Fair point. I set down the knife. "Your brother's in the barn." "I know. I've been watching." "From the barn?" She nodded. "He told me to stay hidden until he knew it was safe." "And you decided it was safe?" "You fed him. You didn't hurt him. You let him sleep inside the second night." She'd been out there the whole time. Three days in the barn, through the cold and the rain, watching the house while Dylan worked in the yard and I pretended I didn't notice the extra set of footprints around the property. "What's your name?" "Lily." "I'm Marta." "I know. Dylan told me." She didn't move from the doorway. Didn't ask to come in, didn't ask for food, didn't do any of the things a child was supposed to do. She just watched, her fingers wrapped around the doorframe. "Are you hungry?" A pause. Then: "Yes." "Sit down. I'll make you something." She sat at the table, her feet barely touching the floor, and I set a plate in front of her—meat, bread, some pickled carrots from a jar I'd found in a basement last month. She ate the way Dylan had: fast, desperate, barely tasting. But when she finished, she didn't look away. She looked at me directly. "It's good," she said. "Thank you." "Dylan doesn't know what it is." "I know." "Are you going to tell him?" I looked at her—this strange, watchful child who asked questions without flinching. "Do you want me to?" She considered this. "I think he already knows. He just doesn't want to say it." "Does that bother you?" "No." She paused. "It used to be people. The meat. But it's not anymore. The spores take everything. What's left is just... food." She said it simply. Like it was obvious. Like it was the kind of thing an eight-year-old should know how to say. I didn't respond. I just took her plate and washed it in the basin, and when I turned around, she was still watching me. "Can I help cook?" she asked. "We'll see." --- Cass arrived that afternoon. I heard her before I saw her—crashing through the underbrush behind the house, making enough noise to wake the dead. Either she didn't know how to move quietly or she didn't care. She emerged at the treeline, and I assessed her from the porch. Forties, going soft in the middle, dressed in too many layers. A backpack sagged off one shoulder. She had a rifle slung over her arm that she clearly didn't know how to use—held it like a purse, not a weapon. "You're making a lot of smoke," she called up. "People can see smoke." "I know." "You're inviting trouble." "Are you trouble?" She stopped. Her face was sharp, worn down by something. Her eyes kept moving—scanning the property, the windows, the barn. "I'm just looking for a place to stay for a night. Then I'll move on." "Dylan said the same thing. He's still here." She looked at the barn, then back at me. "How many of you are there?" "Three, now. Maybe four, if you're staying." She laughed—short, humorless. "You don't even know me." "I know you're tired. I know you haven't eaten in at least two days—I can see it. And I know that rifle isn't loaded, because you're holding it wrong and you haven't checked once to see if I'm armed." Her expression hardened. "I know how to use a gun." "Maybe. But not that one." Silence stretched between us. The wind pushed through the bare trees, cold and damp. "What's the catch?" she asked finally. "I don't know yet. Come inside. I'll feed you. Tomorrow we'll figure out the rest." --- I showed her the root cellar after she ate. I could have lied. Could have pretended the meat was deer, rabbit, anything else. But she was going to stay—I could see it in the way she'd looked at the warm kitchen, the way she'd lingered over the meal—and lies rot faster than truth. The smoked meat hung from hooks in the ceiling, wrapped in cloth. I unwrapped one piece and held it out. "Take a look." She examined it. Turned it over in her hands. I watched her face change as she saw the grain, the color, the faint traces of fungal threads still visible in the fat. "Where did you get this?" "You know where." She was quiet. "It's safe," I said. "The cooking kills the spores. I've been eating it for weeks." "That's not what I asked." "No. It's not." She set the meat down on the shelf carefully, like it might explode. Her hands were shaking. "I was a preschool teacher," she said. "Before. I had twelve kids in my class. Three of them got out. The other nine—" She stopped. Pressed her lips together. "I don't know where they are now. Maybe they're like this. Maybe someone else is—" She didn't finish the sentence. "You don't have to eat it," I said. "There's still some pickled vegetables. A few cans. Not much, but enough if you ration." "How long until that runs out?" "Month. Maybe two." "And then?" I didn't answer. She looked at the meat again. In the dim light of the cellar, her face was all shadows. "I need to think about it," she said. "Take your time." --- That night, the four of us sat at the table together for the first time. Dylan ate without thinking, scraping his plate, going back for seconds. Lily ate with quiet focus, her eyes moving between the faces around her. Cass picked at her food at first, then ate in quick, angry bites. I ate the way I always did now—without letting myself taste too deeply, without letting myself think. We didn't talk much. Dylan tried—asked Cass where she'd come from, made a joke about the weather, filled the silence with words no one needed. Cass answered in short sentences. Lily said nothing at all. The candle between us guttered in a draft. "Was it a person?" Lily asked. Everyone stopped. Dylan's fork hovered halfway to his mouth. Cass went very still. "The meat," Lily clarified. "This one. Was it a person?" No one answered. The silence stretched. I could hear Dylan breathing, could see Cass's knuckles white around her spoon. "We don't know," I said finally. "Maybe. Probably." Lily nodded. "Okay." She took another bite. "Okay?" Dylan's voice cracked. "That's it? Just okay?" "What else is there?" He looked at her—his sister, this strange child he was trying to protect—and couldn't find anything to say. Cass laughed. It was an ugly sound. "We're all going to hell," she said. "Every one of us." "Maybe," I said. "But we'll be fed." No one argued. We finished the meal in silence, and when it was over, I washed the dishes and Dylan banked the fire and Cass disappeared into the room I'd given her without saying goodnight. Lily stayed at the table long after everyone else had gone. I found her there when I came back from the kitchen, staring at the burned-out candle. "Did you mean it?" she asked. "About being fed?" "Yes." "Good." She slid down from the chair and walked to her room, and I listened to her footsteps fade down the hall. Then I sat at the table alone, in the dark, and wondered what I was building.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The writing is clean, economical, and tonally consistent with the established voice—Marta's narration is wry, observant, and deliberately withholding in ways that feel earned rather than coy. The chapter's greatest strength is Lily. Her characterization is genuinely unsettling in the best possible way: she's not precocious in the annoying literary-child sense, but strange in a way that feels psychologically coherent for a child who has survived by becoming a careful observer. Her line "It used to be people. The meat. But it's not anymore. The spores take everything. What's left is just... food" is the chapter's best moment—it lands with quiet horror precisely because it's delivered without affect. The chapter plan called for Lily's "strange presence" and the writing delivers it without over-explaining. The Cass introduction is handled well. The porch confrontation is efficient and characterizes both women economically. Marta's assessment of Cass ("that rifle isn't loaded, because you're holding it wrong and you haven't checked once to see if I'm armed") is sharp and consistent with her observational, controlling personality. The root cellar scene where Marta shows Cass the meat is the chapter's most emotionally complex passage—Cass's unfinished sentence about her students is genuinely affecting, and Marta's pragmatic response ("You don't have to eat it") is exactly right for the character. The communal dinner scene executes the plan's key beat—Lily's question, the silence, the non-answer—with appropriate restraint. The dialogue here is the chapter's most carefully calibrated section. "We're all going to hell. Every one of us." / "Maybe. But we'll be fed." is a strong exchange that earns its darkness without melodrama. Some weaknesses: Dylan remains thin in this chapter. He's present but barely characterized beyond "tries to fill silence, fails." The chapter plan noted his guilt and his protectiveness of Lily, but we get little of that interiority here—he's mostly a reactive presence. The chapter also ends on a slightly too-neat note: "I sat at the table alone, in the dark, and wondered what I was building." This is the one moment where the narration tells rather than shows, and it's a bit on-the-nose for a voice that otherwise trusts the reader. Marta's voice throughout has been defined by not examining her feelings directly, so "wondered what I was building" is slightly out of register. The dialogue is generally strong—spare, functional, with subtext doing real work. The exchange between Marta and Lily about whether Dylan knows what he's eating is particularly good. No character says exactly what they mean, which is consistent with the plan's stated intention. The chapter follows its plan closely: Lily's appearance, Cass's arrival and horrified compromise, the first communal meal, Lily's question, the silence. The plan's beats are all hit. The writing doesn't over-explain the horror or the ethics, which is the correct choice. The prose is clean and avoids purple excess. There are no strained metaphors or forced poetic moments. The one slightly weak image is "lies rot faster than truth"—it's a bit aphoristic and slightly out of register with Marta's otherwise concrete, sensory narration. But it's a minor lapse. The cozy-horror balance is maintained: the warmth of the kitchen, the meal, the candle, the domestic routine—all present and real. The horror is also present and real, held in the same frame without either canceling the other. This is the prompt's central challenge, and the chapter meets it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: Scarcity The root cellar was emptier than I wanted to admit. I stood in the doorway with the lantern held high, counting jars, counting wrapped packages of smoked meat, counting everything twice because the first count didn't match what I remembered. Pickled vegetables: six jars. Canned tomatoes: three. Smoked meat: maybe four pounds if I scraped the margins. The flour was almost gone. The salt was low. The butter had run out last week. Four people. Winter coming. Not enough. I climbed back up the steps and found Dylan in the yard, splitting wood he didn't need to split. He'd been doing that a lot lately—finding tasks to fill the hours, proving himself useful in small, obvious ways. The woodpile was already taller than me. "We need to hunt," I said. The axe stopped mid-swing. "Hunt. You mean—" "Shamblers. Yes." "Right. Yeah. I can do that." He set the axe down carefully, like he was trying to show how responsible he could be. "I've been practicing with the machete. I can—" "Get your coat. We leave in ten minutes." --- He was bad at it. I'd suspected this already, but watching him move through the woods confirmed it. He was loud—stepping on branches, catching his sleeves on thorns, breathing too hard. He held the machete like he was afraid of it, which meant he was afraid of what they were hunting. "Quiet," I said, for the third time. "Sorry. Sorry, I'm trying." "Try less. Move less. Stop apologizing." He swallowed the next apology. His jaw tightened. We moved deeper into the trees. The woods behind the house stretched for miles—old growth, thick underbrush, the kind of terrain that hid things well. I'd mapped some of it during solo supply runs, but there were pockets I'd avoided. Places where the shadows pooled wrong. Places where the spores had settled into the soil and nothing grew anymore. The first shambler appeared in a clearing near the creek. It was alone—unusual, but not unheard of. Sometimes they wandered off from the clusters, drawn by something we couldn't perceive. This one had been a man once. Tall. Broad-shouldered. The fungal growths had bloomed across his chest and throat, pale and fleshy, pulsing faintly in the cold air. I held up a hand. Dylan froze. "Watch the edges of the clearing," I whispered. "Make sure there aren't others." He nodded, his eyes too wide, and scanned the treeline. I could see him counting the shadows, trying to be thorough. "Clear," he breathed. "Stay here." I moved forward, placing each step with care. The wet leaves muffled my boots. The shambler hadn't noticed me yet—its clouded eyes were fixed on something in the middle distance, something only it could see. When I was close enough, I saw what it was wearing. A faded t-shirt, stretched tight across its distorted chest. The design was cracked and peeling, but I could still read the text: *State University*. A logo underneath. A year, maybe—class of something. Someone had graduated. Someone had worn this shirt to games, to parties, to class. Someone had been proud of it. I put the machete through the base of its skull. The body dropped. The shirt was ruined now, split down the middle, soaked in dark blood. Not that it mattered. I wasn't here for the shirt. "Is it dead?" Dylan's voice came from behind me, too loud. "Yes." "Did I—is it safe to come closer?" I didn't answer. I was already crouching over the body, examining the cuts. The thighs were thick. The shoulders, despite the damage, still had usable meat. The fungal growths would need to be cut away carefully. I heard Dylan approach. His footsteps were clumsy, heavy. "Jesus," he said. "That's... it's big." "Help me turn it over." He hesitated. I could hear his breathing, fast and shallow. "Dylan." "Right. Yeah. Sorry." He knelt beside me and grabbed the shoulder—wrong, from the wrong angle, his hands sliding on blood-slicked fabric. The body rolled halfway and then stuck, caught on something. "Push," I said. "I'm trying—" "Harder." He pushed. The body turned. And Dylan's hand pressed directly into the split chest, into the open wound I'd made, his fingers sinking into something wet. He made a sound—not a scream, something more controlled than that. His face went pale. "Don't think about it," I said. "I'm not—I'm fine. I'm fine." His hands were shaking. Blood up to his wrist. He wiped them on his coat, smearing the stain. We field-dressed the body together. I worked the knife while Dylan held the limbs steady, and I could feel the tremor running through him every time I cut. He didn't look at the face. Neither did I. By the time we finished, my back ached and my fingers were numb with cold. The usable meat went into the canvas bag I'd brought. The rest we left for the scavengers. Dylan didn't speak on the walk back. --- Cass was in the kitchen when we returned. She'd been "organizing" again—I could see the evidence immediately. The jars on the shelves had been rearranged by some system I didn't recognize. The pots hung in a different order. My knife block had been moved to the other side of the counter. "You're back," she said. "Obviously." "Get anything?" I set the canvas bag on the table. The meat inside was already cooling, the edges starting to firm. Cass looked at it, then away. "I rearranged the pantry," she said. "You had the beans mixed with the tomatoes. It didn't make sense." "Beans and tomatoes are both canned. That's the system." "Well. Now they're alphabetical." I looked at the shelves. She was right. Alphabetical. "That's not going to work for me," I said. "It's more logical." "It's not how I cook." Cass shrugged, the movement sharp and defensive. "You asked me to be useful. That's useful." I didn't have the energy to fight her. The bag of meat sat between us on the table, dark stains seeping through the canvas. "I'm going to start prep," I said. "Lily can help." "I can help." I looked at Cass—really looked. Her jaw was tight. Her hands were fidgeting with the lighter she always carried, clicking it open and closed. "Fine," I said. "You can wash. Dylan, get the water heated. Lily, I need you to—" "I want to watch the butchering." We all turned. Lily stood in the doorway, small and watchful as always. Her eyes were fixed on the canvas bag. "Excuse me?" Dylan said. "I want to watch," Lily repeated. "When you cut the meat. I want to see how you do it." "Lily, that's not—you don't need to—" "It's fine," I said. "She can watch." Dylan's face twisted. "Marta, she's eight." "She's going to eat it. She might as well understand where it comes from." "That's not—I don't—" Cass laughed. It was an ugly sound. "Let her watch. We're all already damned. Might as well have an audience." --- The meal that night was quiet. I'd prepared the meat simply—seared with salt, finished with the last of the dried herbs. No flourishes. No garnishes. Just protein on a plate, surrounded by pickled vegetables and tough bread. Dylan drank too much of the wine someone had found on a supply run—thin and sour, more vinegar than grape. He refilled his glass twice, then a third time, his movements getting looser and less careful. Cass barely touched her plate. She cut the meat into small pieces, moved them around, took a bite only when she thought I wasn't looking. Lily ate everything. She watched me while she ate, her eyes tracking my hands, my face. The candle guttered in the draft. "This is good," Dylan said finally. His voice was thick. "The meat. It's good." No one responded. "I mean it. Marta, you're—you're really good at this. Whatever you're doing. It's good." "Thank you, Dylan." "I'm not just saying that. I'm—" He stopped. Looked down at his plate. "I'm glad we're here. All of us. Together." Cass's hand tightened around her fork. "That's nice," Lily said. Her voice was flat. "It is. It's—I don't know. It's something. It's better than being out there." "Everything's better than being out there," Cass said. "That's a low bar." "Why do you do that?" Dylan's voice rose. "Every time someone tries to—to make this okay, you just—" "Make what okay?" Cass turned on him. "This? Eating people? Building a little family out of bones and fungus? Pretending we're not all just waiting to—" "Stop." My voice came out harder than I intended. "Both of you. Eat your food or don't. But stop." Silence. Dylan drained his glass. Cass stared at the table. Lily kept eating, methodical and slow. I looked down at my own plate. The meat was half-eaten, cooling, congealing. I'd been hungry for so long. We all had. I finished what was on my plate, and when no one was watching, I went to the kitchen and stood over the basin, gripping the edge of the counter until my knuckles went white. The ring in my pocket pressed against my hip. The house was warm. The table was full. I didn't let myself think about anything at all.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft and largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. **Characterization:** Marta's voice is consistent and well-rendered. Her terseness in dialogue ("Get your coat. We leave in ten minutes"), her physical awareness (the aching back, numb fingers), and her functional relationship to the horror are all true to the profile. The detail of the university t-shirt—noticed, registered, not mentioned to Dylan—is exactly the kind of accumulating human trace the plan called for. Dylan's performed competence collapsing into shaking hands and blood-slicked fingers is well-executed; his drunken attempt at warmth at dinner ("I'm glad we're here. All of us. Together.") is painfully accurate to his character. Lily's request to watch the butchering is genuinely unsettling and consistent with her strange, practical curiosity. Cass's reorganization of the pantry—alphabetical, which is Marta's own system applied wrongly—is a nice touch of friction. Her dinner outburst ("Eating people? Building a little family out of bones and fungus?") is perhaps slightly too on-the-nose, breaking the story's discipline of not naming the thing directly, but it's defensible given her character profile. **Dialogue:** The dialogue is generally strong. It's clipped, functional, and reveals character through what's not said as much as what is. The exchange about the pantry reorganization is particularly good—two people fighting about something that isn't the thing they're fighting about. The dinner argument is the weakest section; Cass's line "Eating people? Building a little family out of bones and fungus?" is the most explicit the story gets about its central horror, and while Cass is the character most likely to say it, it slightly undercuts the story's careful discipline of avoidance. Dylan's drunken sincerity is well-handled. **Prose Quality:** The prose is clean and controlled. The plan called for avoiding aestheticized cooking scenes, and the chapter delivers—the field dressing is practical and ugly, Dylan's hand sinking into the open wound is visceral without being gratuitous. The university t-shirt is the chapter's best moment: "Someone had graduated. Someone had worn this shirt to games, to parties, to class. Someone had been proud of it." This is restrained and effective. The chapter avoids purple prose almost entirely. There are no forced metaphors or questionable poetic leaps. **Pacing and Structure:** The chapter moves well through its planned beats: Dylan's inadequacy exposed, the university shirt unremarked (though it is remarked upon internally, which is appropriate), the friction with Cass, Lily's strange interest, the weight settling over the table. The ending—Marta gripping the counter, the ring pressing against her hip, "I didn't let myself think about anything at all"—is earned and consistent with her character. **Following the Plan:** The chapter follows its plan closely. The university shirt is noticed but not pointed out to Dylan (though the plan says "Dylan doesn't seem to notice, or chooses not to"—here Dylan doesn't comment, which works). Cass's "organizing" is rendered as criticism. Dylan drinks too much. Lily watches with genuine interest. The weight of what they're doing settles over the table. The plan's note about Cass staring at her plate is delivered. One minor deviation: the plan mentions "the bad wine someone found" and Dylan drinking too much—this is executed well. **Weaknesses:** The dinner argument is the chapter's weakest moment. Cass naming the horror explicitly ("Eating people") breaks the story's careful discipline, and while it's in character, it slightly cheapens the accumulated tension. The resolution—Marta shutting them down with "Both of you. Eat your food or don't. But stop."—is a bit too clean. The ending, while effective, is perhaps slightly predictable given the established pattern of Marta suppressing and enduring. **Overall:** This is a well-executed chapter that serves the larger work faithfully. It's not flashy, but it's precise, and precision is what this story requires. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: Birthday Dylan found me in the root cellar three days later. "I need flour," he said. "And whatever sugar you have left. And—do we have any dried fruit? Raisins? Cranberries?" I looked up from the jar I was labeling. "Why?" " Lily's birthday. It's tomorrow. She's turning nine." The words landed wrong. I'd been counting days—or trying not to count them—and I'd missed this completely. The weight of it pressed down on my chest: another thing I should have known, should have remembered, should have been paying attention to. "I didn't know," I said. "I didn't expect you to." He was already moving, scanning the shelves with desperate focus. "I just want to make something. A cake or—or something. It doesn't have to be good. It just has to be a cake." I looked at his face—his eager, anxious face—and felt the gap between what he was asking for and what I could give. "The flour's almost gone," I said. "But there's some. I've been saving it. Sugar too, what's left of the peaches from the jar in the back—they're sweet enough. And there's dried apples in the second cabinet. Not much, but—" "Thank you." The relief in his voice was painful. "Thank you, Marta. I just—I want her to have something. One normal thing." Normal. I swallowed the word before it could escape my throat. --- The cake was ugly. I made it the next morning while Dylan took Lily outside—"just a walk, I promise, we'll be back in an hour"—and the process was an exercise in compromise. The flour was coarse and gray. The dried apples had to be chopped fine, rehydrated with water I heated on the stove. I didn't have baking powder anymore, so I used the last of the baking soda and hoped the acid from the peaches would be enough. The result rose unevenly, lumpy and dense. I cut off the burned edges and arranged the peaches on top like decoration. It wasn't a cake. It was an approximation, a gesture. It would have to be enough. --- The main dish was better. I'd been saving the last of my good spices—cumin, coriander, a single cinnamon stick I'd found in a house three towns over. The meat I selected was from the loin, the most tender cut I had left. I braised it slowly, with the last of my salt and the dried herbs I'd foraged in late summer, and when the smell filled the kitchen, it almost felt like before. I set the table carefully. The cloth napkins. The good dishes. Two candles this time—one I'd been saving, and another I'd found in Cass's room when she wasn't looking. She wouldn't notice. She never noticed anything that wasn't directly in front of her. When Dylan and Lily came back, I heard them before I saw them—Lily laughing, a sound I didn't recognize. It was strange and bright, and it made something in my chest ache. "Close your eyes," Dylan was saying. "No peeking—I see you peeking." "I'm not peeking!" "Your eyes are open. I can tell." "Only a little!" He guided her through the door, his hands on her shoulders. When she opened her eyes, she saw the table—the candles, the food, the ugly little cake in the center. Her face didn't change. She just stood there, looking. "Lily?" Dylan's voice was uncertain. "Do you like it?" "Is this for me?" "All of it. It's your birthday. Nine years old." She stepped forward slowly. Her hand reached out, touched the edge of the table, traced the pattern on the cloth napkin. "I don't—" Her voice caught. "I don't remember my last birthday." "I know," Dylan said softly. "I know, Lils." "The spores came before. And then Mom and Dad—" She stopped. Drew a breath. "I don't remember." "We're making a new one," Dylan said. "Right here. New birthday. New memory." She looked at him, then at me, then at Cass, who was hovering in the doorway with an expression I couldn't read. "Okay," Lily said. "Okay." --- The dinner started well. We ate. The meat was good—rich and tender, the spices warming the back of the throat. Lily took small bites, chewing carefully, savoring. Dylan watched her with a desperate kind of hope, like if she enjoyed it, everything else would fall into place. "This is really good," Lily said. "The best one yet." I nodded. Acknowledged the compliment. Didn't let myself feel it. "Happy birthday, Lily," Cass said. Her voice was careful. Measured. "Nine is a good age." "Is it?" "I had a student once. Turned nine the week before—" She stopped. Her hand went to her wine glass, found it empty, went still. "Never mind." Dylan shot her a look. She ignored him. "Can I have more?" Lily asked. "Of the meat?" "Of course." I served her another portion. My hands were steady. I made sure of it. "Can I have seconds of cake too?" Lily asked. "After?" "Save room." "Okay." She smiled—small, private. "Okay." --- It was after the cake that things went wrong. Dylan had found something to give her—a hair clip he'd been saving, shaped like a butterfly, the paint chipped but still visible. Lily held it in her palm like it was made of gold. "I found it when I was scavenging," he said. "I thought—it looked like something you'd like." "I love it." Her voice was thick. She looked up at him, and for a moment, her face was open. "Thank you, Dylan." He pulled her into a hug, fierce and tight. She let him. Cass watched from across the table. She'd found more wine somewhere—another bottle, discovered God knows where. Her glass was half-empty, and when she set it down, the sound was too loud. "We're going to hell, you know." The words fell like stones. Dylan looked up, his arms still around Lily. "What?" "All of us." Cass's voice was clear despite the wine. "Every single one of us. Eating people. Making birthday cakes out of—" She gestured at the table, the plates, the food. "This." "Cass, don't—" " Don't what? Don't say it out loud? Don't remind you what we're celebrating with?" She laughed—sharp, humorless. "Your sister is nine years old, and she's eaten more human flesh than most people see in a lifetime. And you're making birthday cake." "Shut up." Dylan's voice cracked. "Just—shut up." "I'm not wrong." "You're not helping." I said it quietly, but the words carried. "Whatever point you're trying to make, this isn't the time." "There's no good time." Cass stood. Her chair scraped against the floor. "There's no good time to talk about what we are. What we've become. So why not at a birthday party?" Lily was still holding the hair clip. She wasn't looking at anyone—just the butterfly in her palm, the chipped paint, the broken wings. "I know what we are," she said softly. Everyone stopped. "I know what we eat. I know what it used to be." She looked up at Cass, her eyes too steady for a nine-year-old. "I'm not stupid. I'm not pretending. I just don't know what else to do." No one answered. "Should we stop?" Lily asked. "Should we starve? Should we die instead?" "Lily—" Dylan's voice broke. "I'm asking. I want to know. Because everyone keeps acting like there's another choice, but I don't see one. Do you?" The silence stretched. Cass sat back down. She didn't look at anyone. Her hand found her wine glass again, lifted it, drained it. "No," she said finally. "I don't." --- After dinner, I cut Lily a second piece of cake. My hands shook as I held the knife—just slightly, a tremor I couldn't control. I watched my own fingers curl around the handle, watched the blade press into the dense crumb, and I couldn't tell if the shaking was from exhaustion or something else. Lily took the plate without looking at me. She ate quietly, methodically, the same way she always did. Dylan sat beside her, his hand on her shoulder, his face drawn. Cass had disappeared into her room without a word. The candles had burned down to stubs, the wax pooling on the table. I should have cleaned up. Should have washed the dishes, banked the fire, done all the small tasks that kept a house running. Instead, I sat at the table and watched Lily eat, and thought about her question: *Should we stop?* I didn't have an answer. None of us did. When Lily finished, she carried her plate to the kitchen, rinsed it in the basin, and set it on the rack to dry. Then she went to her room without saying goodnight. Dylan followed, his footsteps heavy on the floorboards. I stayed at the table, in the dark, until the last candle guttered out.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and restraint. The writing maintains the voice established in the planning document—Marta's wry, functional, food-focused perspective—while delivering the emotional beats the chapter promised: the warmth of celebration, Cass's destabilizing honesty, and Lily's devastating clarity. The strongest element is Lily's dialogue during the confrontation. "I know what we eat. I know what it used to be. I'm not stupid. I'm not pretending. I just don't know what else to do" and "Should we stop? Should we starve? Should we die instead?" are genuinely earned moments. The plan called for Lily to be the one who cuts through the silence, and the execution delivers something more precise than the plan anticipated—Lily doesn't just ask a question, she dismantles the moral theater everyone else is performing. This is the chapter's best writing. The cake-making sequence is appropriately unglamorous. "The flour was coarse and gray" and the description of the ugly, compromised result avoids the aestheticized cooking the plan warned against. The practical ugliness is right. Cass's "We're going to hell" speech lands as planned, though the execution is slightly on-the-nose. The plan called for her to say true things at wrong moments, and she does, but her dialogue here is perhaps too articulate about its own purpose—"Don't say it out loud? Don't remind you what we're celebrating with?" reads as slightly expository, as if she's explaining her own function in the narrative. Real Cass, per the profile, says the quiet thing out loud without framing it so neatly. The opening exchange between Marta and Dylan about the flour and sugar is well-handled. Dylan's "one normal thing" and Marta swallowing the word "normal" is efficient and effective. The chapter doesn't over-explain this irony, which is the right call. Some weaknesses: The transition into the dinner scene is slightly rushed. "The dinner started well" is a functional but flat header-sentence that tells rather than shows. The chapter plan called for Dylan drinking too much of the bad wine, but this is transferred to Cass, which is consistent with her character but loses the specific beat about Dylan's coping. The plan also specified Marta's hands shaking as she cuts Lily a second piece of cake, which is delivered, but the moment is slightly over-narrated: "I watched my own fingers curl around the handle, watched the blade press into the dense crumb, and I couldn't tell if the shaking was from exhaustion or something else." The uncertainty is real, but the sentence draws too much attention to itself. The ending—Marta sitting in the dark until the candle gutters—is effective but slightly conventional. The plan's final image of Marta's hand shaking as she lifts her spoon is more specific and more resonant than what's delivered here. The chapter ends on Marta's isolation rather than on the continuation-despite-everything that the plan envisioned. The dialogue is generally strong. Lily's voice is consistent with her profile—the impossible questions, the practical framing of horror. Dylan's voice is slightly less distinctive here than in his profile; his "New birthday. New memory" is a bit too clean, too therapeutic. The profile describes him as someone who fills silence with chatter and lies about small things; this Dylan is more composed than expected. Coherence is high. The chapter follows its plan closely, hits its key beats, and maintains tonal consistency. The horror and warmth coexist without either canceling the other, which is the central tonal challenge of the piece. The writing avoids purple prose almost entirely. There are no strained metaphors, no decorative language that doesn't earn its place. The prose is appropriately spare and functional, matching Marta's voice. Overall, this is a strong chapter that delivers on its promises. Its weaknesses are minor: a slightly over-explained Cass, a slightly under-specific ending, and one or two moments where the narration tells what it should show. But the central achievement—Lily's confrontation, the warmth-and-wrongness held together, the characters consistent with their profiles—is real. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7: Deep Winter January was the longest month. The cold settled into the house like a living thing—creeping through the floorboards, nesting in the walls, pressing against the windows until the glass frosted thick. We slept in layers, all of us crowded into the living room where the woodstove could reach, our breath visible in the mornings. The root cellar was almost empty now. Four jars of pickled vegetables. Two cans of tomatoes. The smoked meat was down to scraps—stringy pieces I'd been saving for broth, fat I'd been rendering for oil. We rationed carefully, weighing portions, counting calories I didn't have the energy to calculate. The shamblers had stopped coming. I didn't know if they hibernated or migrated or simply died off in the cold. All I knew was that the woods had gone quiet. No rustling in the underbrush. No wet, rattling breath in the darkness. Just silence and snow and the slow, gnawing ache of being hungry all the time. Dylan hunted every day. He came back with nothing—red-faced, shivering, his hands too stiff to hold the machete properly. I watched him get slower, weaker. I watched Lily's wrists thin to sticks. I watched Cass stop moving entirely, spending whole days in her room with the door closed. I didn't know how to fix any of it. --- The argument started on a Tuesday. I was in the kitchen, trying to make broth from bones I'd already used twice, when Cass appeared in the doorway.
